knowledge.what_it_is: Python is a high-level, interpreted programming language widely used in DevSecOps for automation, scripting, and configuration management tasks due to its simplicity, flexibility, and extensive libraries. It's particularly popular in CI/CD pipelines, container orchestration, and infrastructure as code (IaC) tools.
what_you_will_learn: Python syntax basics — understanding indentation, variables, data types, control structures, functions, and modules
Package management with pip — installing, updating, and managing dependencies using requirements.txt files
Virtual environments with virtualenv or conda — isolating project dependencies for reproducibility and portability
Automation with Ansible and Fabric — using Python to automate system administration tasks, configuration management, and deployment
CI/CD pipeline automation with Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, or CircleCI — leveraging Python scripts to trigger builds, tests, and deployments
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) with Terraform or CloudFormation — using Python to define and manage cloud infrastructure resources
Containerization with Docker — writing Python scripts to build, push, and run containerized applications
Security best practices for Python development — understanding secure coding guidelines, input validation, and error handling
Logging and monitoring with ELK Stack or Splunk — using Python to collect, process, and visualize log data
subtopics: logging and monitoring with elk stack

knowledge.what_it_is: Go, also known as Golang, is a statically typed, compiled language developed by Google to build scalable and concurrent systems. It's designed to be efficient, lightweight, and easy to learn, making it an ideal choice for DevSecOps engineers who need to write high-performance code.
what_you_will_learn: Go Modules — managing dependencies with the go mod command and go.sum file
GOPATH and GOROOT environment variables — configuring Go's build process
go get command — downloading and installing packages from the Go repository
goroutine and channel basics — writing concurrent code using goroutines and channels
Mutexes and RWMutexes — synchronizing access to shared resources with mutexes
Error handling with errors.New() and defer statements — propagating errors in Go
Go's context package — canceling and timing out goroutines with Context
go test command — writing and running unit tests for Go code
Go's net/http package — building web servers and clients using the HTTP protocol

knowledge.what_it_is: JavaScript and Node.js are used to build scalable, high-performance server-side applications that integrate with DevOps tools and practices.
what_you_will_learn: Node.js — a JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine for building scalable server-side apps
Express.js — a popular web framework for Node.js providing routing, middleware, and template engines
Koa.js — an enterprise-level web framework for Node.js offering better performance and flexibility
npm and yarn package managers — installing, managing dependencies, and versioning packages
ES6+ syntax and features — let, const, arrow functions, classes, async/await, Promises
Node.js modules and exports — importing and exporting modules, creating reusable code
Error handling and logging with Winston, Morgan, or Bunyan
Security best practices for Node.js apps, including input validation, sanitization, and secure password storage

knowledge.what_it_is: Ruby is a high-level, dynamic language used extensively in DevSecOps for automation and scripting tasks due to its simplicity, readability, and extensive libraries. It's particularly popular in the industry for writing scripts that interact with infrastructure as code tools like Terraform, Ansible, and CloudFormation.
what_you_will_learn: Ruby syntax basics — understanding variables, control structures, loops, and functions
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) concepts in Ruby — classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism
Modules and Namespaces in Ruby — organizing code with modules, requiring libraries, and avoiding naming conflicts
Hashes and Arrays in Ruby — working with data structures for configuration management and automation
Ruby's built-in networking libraries (Net::HTTP, Net::FTP) — making HTTP requests, uploading files to S3
Using Rake for build automation and dependency management — creating custom tasks, defining dependencies
Ruby's support for parallel processing with threads and fibers — improving script performance in DevSecOps pipelines

knowledge.what_it_is: Rust is a systems programming language that focuses on memory safety, performance, and concurrency, designed to replace C and C++ in many use cases. It's used by companies like Amazon, Google, and Microsoft for building operating systems, file systems, and other low-level system components.
what_you_will_learn: Ownership — Rust's core concept that ensures memory safety through a borrow checker
Borrowing and lending — understanding how to share resources between functions and threads
Smart pointers — using Box, Rc, and Arc for automatic memory management
Error handling — using Result and Option for error propagation and handling
Traits and generics — defining reusable abstractions for data structures and algorithms
Pattern matching — using match statements for concise and expressive code
Modules and crates — organizing code into reusable packages with dependencies
Rust's standard library — learning the core APIs for strings, collections, and I/O

knowledge.what_it_is: Windows is a widely-used operating system developed by Microsoft, commonly used in enterprise environments and DevSecOps pipelines due to its robust security features and integration with Azure services.
what_you_will_learn: WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux) — installing and configuring Linux distributions on Windows for seamless development and testing
Azure Disk Encryption — using BitLocker to encrypt Windows VMs and disks, integrating with Azure Key Vault for secure key management
Group Policy Objects (GPOs) — creating and applying centralized policies for user and system settings, including security and software deployment
Windows Defender Advanced Threat Protection (ATP) — detecting and responding to advanced threats in real-time using machine learning-based analytics
PowerShell Desired State Configuration (DSC) — managing Windows configurations using PowerShell scripts and DSC resources for automation and compliance
Azure Active Directory (AAD) integration — authenticating and authorizing users and services with Azure AD, enabling single sign-on and conditional access
Windows Server Core — deploying a minimal Windows installation for improved security and reduced attack surface
System Center Configuration Manager (SCCM) — managing Windows updates, patches, and software deployments using SCCM's robust features

knowledge.what_it_is: Bash is a Unix shell and command-line interpreter, widely used in DevSecOps for automation, scripting, and configuration management. It provides a powerful way to execute commands, manage files, and interact with other tools.
what_you_will_learn: Variables — declaring and using variables in Bash, including environment variables and parameter expansion
Conditional statements — writing if-else statements, case statements, and conditional loops (for, while)
Functions — defining and calling reusable functions to simplify code and reduce repetition
Arrays — working with arrays, including indexing, slicing, and iterating over elements
Loops — using for, while, and until loops to iterate over collections and execute repetitive tasks
Regular expressions — using grep, sed, and awk to search, replace, and manipulate text patterns
File management — creating, reading, writing, and deleting files and directories, including permissions and ownership
Process management — running background jobs, managing process IDs, and controlling job scheduling

knowledge.what_it_is: FreeBSD is a free and open-source Unix-like operating system used as a base for various DevSecOps tools, including containerization platforms like Docker. It provides a stable and secure foundation for building and deploying applications.
what_you_will_learn: Package management using pkg — installing and managing packages with the pkg tool
Ports collection — using the ports system to install software from source code
FreeBSD jails — creating isolated environments for running multiple services or applications
ZFS file system — managing storage with the ZFS file system, including snapshots and replication
Firewall configuration using pf — setting up firewall rules with the pf tool
Secure Shell (SSH) server setup — configuring SSH access to FreeBSD systems
FreeBSD kernel configuration — customizing the kernel for specific hardware or performance needs

knowledge.what_it_is: Ubuntu and Debian are popular Linux distributions used extensively in DevSecOps environments, known for their stability, security features, and package management systems like apt.
what_you_will_learn: Package Management — managing dependencies with apt, apt-get, and dpkg
apt Sources List — configuring repositories for package updates and installations
SSH Server Configuration — securing remote access with public key authentication and passwordless login
Firewall Configuration — setting up ufw to control incoming and outgoing traffic
System Update and Patching — keeping systems secure with apt update, upgrade, and dist-upgrade
User and Group Management — creating and managing users with adduser and groupadd
Filesystem Hierarchy — understanding the structure of the Linux filesystem
Log Rotation and Management — configuring logrotate to manage system logs

knowledge.what_it_is: OpenBSD is a free and open-source operating system designed with security as its primary focus, developed by Theo de Raadt and his team. It is known for its robust security features, including mandatory access control (MAC) and intrusion detection.
what_you_will_learn: Installing OpenBSD — understanding the installation process, including partitioning and boot loader configuration
Configuring network interfaces — setting up IP addresses, netmasks, gateways, and DNS servers using ifconfig and rc.conf
Managing users and groups — creating and managing user accounts, groups, and permissions using pw and group commands
Setting up OpenBSD firewall — configuring pf (packet filter) to control incoming and outgoing network traffic
Understanding OpenBSD's security features — learning about MAC, intrusion detection, and other security mechanisms built into the OS
Configuring OpenBSD for remote access — setting up SSH, rsh, and other remote access protocols using authorized_keys and known_hosts files

knowledge.what_it_is: SUSE Linux is an enterprise-grade, open-source operating system developed by SUSE, a leading provider of software-defined infrastructure and application delivery solutions. It is designed to provide a secure, reliable, and scalable platform for DevSecOps teams to deploy and manage applications.
what_you_will_learn: Package management with zypper — managing packages, dependencies, and repositories in SUSE Linux
YAST (Yet another Setup Tool) configuration — configuring network settings, user accounts, and system services using YAST
SMBus support — enabling communication between devices on the same bus using SMBus protocol
SELinux integration — enforcing mandatory access control policies to secure the system
Kubernetes deployment with kubic — deploying containerized applications on SUSE Linux using Kubernetes
OpenSCAP compliance scanning — scanning and reporting on system security configuration and vulnerabilities
SUSE Manager for SLES (SUSE Linux Enterprise Server) — managing multiple SUSE Linux systems from a central location

knowledge.what_it_is: NetBSD is a free and open-source Unix-like operating system, designed to be highly portable and secure. It is widely used in embedded systems, servers, and cloud infrastructure due to its small footprint, high performance, and robust security features.
what_you_will_learn: Package management using pkgin — a wrapper for the NetBSD package manager, pkgsrc
System configuration with rc.conf — managing system services and settings
User and group management with pw and grp — creating and modifying user accounts and groups
Network configuration with ifconfig and netstat — setting up network interfaces and monitoring network connections
Firewall configuration with pf — configuring packet filtering and firewall rules
Security hardening using the NetBSD Security Checklist — implementing best practices for secure system configuration

knowledge.what_it_is: Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) and its derivatives are enterprise-grade Linux distributions used for server, desktop, and cloud environments. They provide a stable, secure, and scalable platform for DevSecOps teams to manage infrastructure, applications, and services.
what_you_will_learn: Package management with YUM/DNF — managing packages, dependencies, and repositories
System configuration using Ansible — automating system setup, deployment, and management
SELinux (Security-Enhanced Linux) — enforcing mandatory access control policies for security
RHEL subscription management — understanding licensing, entitlements, and compliance requirements
Kubernetes on RHEL — deploying containerized applications on RHEL-based clusters
System hardening with SCAP (Security Content Automation Protocol) — automating security configuration and vulnerability scanning
RHEL derivatives like CentOS Stream/AlmaLinux — understanding the differences and use cases for each variant

knowledge.what_it_is: PowerShell is a task automation and configuration management framework from Microsoft, used by DevSecOps engineers to automate system administration tasks, manage infrastructure, and implement security policies. It's widely adopted in Windows environments but also supports Linux and macOS.
what_you_will_learn: Cmdlets — PowerShell commands that perform specific actions (e.g., Get-Process, Set-Location)
Modules — reusable collections of cmdlets for specific tasks (e.g., ActiveDirectory, ExchangeOnline)
Powershell DSC (Desired State Configuration) — declarative configuration management using PowerShell scripts
PowerShell Remoting — remote execution and management of PowerShell sessions on multiple machines
PowerShell Profiles — customizing the PowerShell environment with user-specific settings and functions
PowerShell Scripting — writing reusable, modular scripts for automation and orchestration tasks
PowerShell Security Best Practices — securing PowerShell environments, managing permissions, and preventing lateral movement

knowledge.what_it_is: Process monitoring is the practice of continuously observing and analyzing system performance to identify bottlenecks, optimize resource utilization, and ensure overall system reliability. In DevSecOps, process monitoring is crucial for detecting security threats, identifying vulnerabilities, and ensuring compliance with organizational policies.
what_you_will_learn: Prometheus — a popular open-source monitoring system for collecting metrics from various sources, including applications, services, and infrastructure
Grafana — a visualization tool for creating dashboards to display Prometheus metrics, enabling real-time monitoring and alerting
Service Discovery — the process of automatically detecting and registering services in a distributed system, using tools like Consul or etcd
Container Monitoring — tracking container performance, resource utilization, and logs using tools like Docker Metrics or Kubernetes Dashboard
Anomaly Detection — identifying unusual patterns in system behavior using machine learning algorithms and techniques like statistical process control
Alerting and Notification — configuring alerts for critical events, such as high CPU usage or failed services, to notify DevOps teams via email, Slack, or PagerDuty
Log Analysis — collecting, processing, and analyzing log data from various sources to identify security threats, troubleshoot issues, and optimize system performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Performance monitoring involves tracking and analyzing system performance metrics to identify bottlenecks, optimize resource utilization, and ensure high availability. In DevSecOps, it's crucial to monitor application performance, infrastructure health, and security posture.
what_you_will_learn: Prometheus — a popular open-source monitoring system for collecting and storing metrics from various sources
Grafana — a visualization tool for creating dashboards and charts to display performance data
New Relic — an all-in-one monitoring platform for application, infrastructure, and security performance
Docker Monitoring — tracking container resource utilization, network I/O, and process metrics
Kubernetes Metrics — collecting and analyzing pod, node, and cluster-level performance metrics
eBPF (Extended Berkeley Packet Filter) — a Linux kernel technology for monitoring network traffic and system calls
Systemd CGroups — managing and monitoring container resource allocation and utilization
Linux Perf — a tool for profiling and analyzing CPU, memory, and I/O performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Networking tools are software applications used to monitor, manage, and troubleshoot network infrastructure, ensuring secure and efficient communication between devices.
what_you_will_learn: Nmap — scanning for open ports and services on remote hosts
Tcpdump — capturing and analyzing network traffic in real-time
Wireshark — dissecting packet captures to identify network issues
Netcat — creating network connections and tunnels between systems
Nslookup — resolving DNS queries and troubleshooting domain name resolution
Dig — performing DNS lookups and verifying DNS server configurations
Telnet — establishing remote access to network devices for management and configuration
Ssh — securely accessing network devices using secure shell protocol

knowledge.what_it_is: Text manipulation refers to the process of modifying, transforming, and processing text data within a DevSecOps pipeline. This involves using various tools and techniques to extract insights, automate tasks, and ensure security compliance.
what_you_will_learn: Regular expressions — powerful patterns for matching and extracting text data from logs, configuration files, and other sources
String manipulation in Python — using libraries like re, string, and f-strings to perform operations on text strings within DevSecOps scripts
Text encoding and decoding — understanding character encodings (UTF-8, ASCII), decoding mechanisms (Base64, URL encoding), and handling special characters in text data
Log parsing and processing — using tools like Logstash, Fluentd, or Splunk to extract relevant information from log files and transform it into a usable format
Text normalization and standardization — techniques for removing noise, converting formats, and ensuring consistency in text data across different sources
Natural language processing (NLP) fundamentals — understanding concepts like tokenization, stemming, lemmatization, and named entity recognition to extract insights from unstructured text data

knowledge.what_it_is: Vim, Nano, and Emacs are three popular text editors used by DevSecOps professionals to efficiently manage and edit configuration files, scripts, and other code snippets. They offer advanced features such as syntax highlighting, auto-completion, and customization through plugins.
what_you_will_learn: Vim navigation — mastering the 'h', 'j', 'k', 'l' keys for efficient movement within the editor
Nano basic commands — understanding how to create, save, and exit files using Nano's simple interface
Emacs key bindings — learning the basics of Emacs keyboard shortcuts, including C-x, C-c, and C-v
Vim plugins — installing and configuring popular Vim plugins such as vim-airline, vim-fugitive, and nerdtree
Nano configuration files — understanding how to customize Nano's behavior through its configuration file
Emacs packages — managing and installing Emacs packages using MELPA or ELPA repositories
Vim syntax highlighting — configuring Vim to highlight specific programming languages such as Python, Java, or C++
Nano keyboard shortcuts — mastering Nano's keyboard shortcuts for efficient editing and navigation

knowledge.what_it_is: Git is a free and open-source distributed version control system created by Linus Torvalds, widely used in DevSecOps to manage code changes across multiple developers and environments.
what_you_will_learn: Git repositories — creating and managing centralized or decentralized repositories for code collaboration
Branching strategies — implementing feature branches, release branches, hotfix branches, and merge requests for efficient code management
Commit messages — writing clear and concise commit messages following the Conventional Commits format for better code readability and auditability
Git tags — creating and managing version tags to track software releases and milestones
Git hooks — setting up pre-commit, pre-push, and post-receive hooks for automated code validation and deployment
Git merge strategies — understanding and implementing different merge strategies (e.g., fast-forward, recursive) for conflict resolution
Git stash — using the git stash command to temporarily save changes and switch between branches
Git rebase — rebasing commits to maintain a linear commit history and resolve conflicts

knowledge.what_it_is: GitHub is a web-based platform for version control and collaboration on software development projects, used by DevSecOps teams to manage code repositories, track changes, and automate workflows.
what_you_will_learn: Repository management — creating, cloning, and managing Git repositories using GitHub's web interface or CLI tools like gh
Branching strategies — understanding the concept of feature branches, release branches, and hotfix branches in a DevSecOps context
Pull requests — reviewing and merging code changes using GitHub's pull request workflow and automated testing tools like GitHub Actions
Code reviews — participating in code review processes using GitHub's built-in features and third-party tools like Codecov
Dependency management — managing dependencies using GitHub Packages, npm, or yarn, and understanding the importance of dependency updates in DevSecOps
Security scanning — integrating security scanning tools like Snyk, Dependabot, or GitHub Security Advisories to identify vulnerabilities in dependencies
Automated testing — setting up automated testing workflows using GitHub Actions, CircleCI, or Jenkins to ensure code quality and reliability
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines — configuring CI/CD pipelines using GitHub Actions, AWS CodePipeline, or Google Cloud Build to automate deployment and delivery

knowledge.what_it_is: GitLab is an all-in-one DevOps platform that provides source code management, continuous integration and deployment (CI/CD), and issue tracking features. It's widely used by DevSecOps teams for its ease of use, scalability, and flexibility.
what_you_will_learn: Creating a new project in GitLab — understanding the different types of projects (public, private, internal) and how to create them from scratch
Setting up CI/CD pipelines in GitLab — using .gitlab-ci.yml files to define build, test, and deployment stages for automated workflows
Using GitLab's built-in container registry — storing and managing Docker images, and integrating with Kubernetes clusters

Implementing GitLab's security features — understanding how to use SAST (Static Application Security Testing), DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing), and IAST (Interactive Application Security Testing) for vulnerability detection
Configuring GitLab's audit logging and compliance features — setting up log retention policies, and integrating with external compliance tools like SOX and PCI-DSS
Using GitLab's issue tracking and project management features — creating and assigning issues, epics, and milestones to track progress and collaborate with team members

knowledge.what_it_is: Bitbucket is a web-based source code repository manager hosted by Atlassian, used for version control and collaboration on software development projects.
what_you_will_learn: Repository management — creating, cloning, and managing repositories with Bitbucket Cloud or Server
Branching and merging strategies — using GitFlow, GitHub Flow, or other workflows to manage feature branches and releases
Code reviews and approvals — configuring code review policies, assigning reviewers, and approving changes
Pull requests and merges — creating, reviewing, and merging pull requests with Bitbucket's built-in features
Access control and permissions — managing user roles, permissions, and access levels for repositories and projects
Webhooks and integrations — setting up webhooks to trigger external actions, such as sending notifications or updating other tools
CI/CD pipeline integration — integrating Bitbucket with CI/CD tools like Jenkins, Travis CI, or CircleCI

knowledge.what_it_is: LXC (Linux Containers) is a lightweight virtualization technology that allows multiple isolated environments to run on a single Linux host, providing process-level isolation and resource management. It's a key component of DevSecOps for building and deploying containerized applications.
what_you_will_learn: Container creation — creating new LXC containers using lxc-create, specifying rootfs, network settings, and other parameters
Container configuration — managing container configurations, including networking, storage, and security settings through lxc.conf
Container lifecycle management — starting, stopping, and restarting LXC containers using lxc-start, lxc-stop, and lxc-restart
Resource allocation and isolation — configuring CPU, memory, and I/O resources for LXC containers to ensure proper isolation and utilization
Networking in LXC — setting up network interfaces, bridges, and VLANs for LXC containers to enable communication with the host and other containers
Security features of LXC — understanding how LXC provides security through process-level isolation, SELinux, and AppArmor
LXC and Docker integration — using lxc-docker to integrate LXC with Docker, enabling seamless container management and orchestration

knowledge.what_it_is: Docker is an open-source containerization platform that allows developers to package, ship, and run applications in containers across various environments, promoting consistency, portability, and efficiency. It is widely adopted in DevSecOps for its ability to simplify application deployment, reduce dependencies, and improve security.
what_you_will_learn: Containerization — the process of packaging an application and its dependencies into a single container
Docker Images — read-only templates that contain the application code, libraries, and settings
Docker Containers — running instances of Docker images with their own file system, processes, and network stack
Docker Hub — a public registry for sharing and retrieving Docker images
Docker Compose — a tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications
Dockerfile — a text file that contains instructions for building a Docker image
Multi-stage builds — optimizing Docker images by separating build and runtime dependencies
Layer caching — improving Docker build performance by reusing cached layers
Docker volumes — persistent storage solutions for containers, allowing data to persist between restarts
Docker networking — managing container-to-container communication using Docker's built-in network stack

topics.section_name: What is and how to setup X ?

knowledge.what_it_is: Nginx is an open-source, high-performance web server and reverse proxy software that can be used to improve the security, scalability, and reliability of web applications. It supports HTTP/2, SSL/TLS encryption, and load balancing.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring Nginx as a reverse proxy — using the `proxy_pass` directive to forward requests from one server to another
Setting up SSL/TLS encryption with Let's Encrypt — using the `ssl_certificate` and `ssl_certificate_key` directives to secure connections
Implementing load balancing with Nginx — using the `upstream` block to distribute traffic across multiple servers
Configuring caching with Nginx — using the `expires` and `etag` directives to improve performance by reducing the number of requests made to the origin server
Securing Nginx with firewall rules — using the `http` block to configure access control lists (ACLs) and restrict incoming traffic
Monitoring Nginx logs — using tools like Loggly or Splunk to collect, analyze, and visualize log data for troubleshooting and security purposes

topics.section_name: What is and how to setup X ?

knowledge.what_it_is: Caddy is an open-source, reverse proxy web server and HTTP/3 accelerator that provides automated HTTPS (SSL/TLS) and automatic configuration of various protocols such as HTTP/2, WebSocket, and WebSockets. It's designed to be highly customizable and extensible.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring Caddy with JSON or TOML files — defining server blocks, routes, and middleware
Automated HTTPS (SSL/TLS) configuration using Let's Encrypt ACME protocol — obtaining certificates, renewals, and challenges
Reverse proxying with Caddy — routing requests from external clients to internal servers, load balancing, and caching
HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 support — enabling modern web protocols for improved performance and security
WebSocket and WebSockets support — enabling bidirectional communication between clients and servers
Middleware customization using Go plugins — extending Caddy's functionality with custom logic and integrations
Monitoring and logging with Caddy — integrating with external tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and ELK Stack

topics.section_name: What is and how to setup X ?

knowledge.what_it_is: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) and SFTP (Secure File Transfer Protocol) are protocols used to transfer files over a network, with FTP being insecure and SFTP providing secure file transfers. In DevSecOps, understanding FTP/SFTP is crucial for securely transferring sensitive data between systems.
what_you_will_learn: FTP vs SFTP — understanding the differences in security features and usage
SFTP server setup using OpenSSH (sshd) on Linux/Unix platforms
SFTP client configuration using WinSCP, FileZilla, or Cyberduck for Windows/Linux/Mac
Using public key authentication with SFTP to secure file transfers
Configuring SFTP server-side settings for permissions, access control, and logging
Implementing SFTP tunneling through SSH for secure file transfer over unsecured networks
Troubleshooting common FTP/SFTP issues such as connection timeouts, permission errors, and authentication failures

topics.section_name: What is and how to setup X ?

knowledge.what_it_is: DNS (Domain Name System) is a critical component of DevSecOps, responsible for translating human-readable domain names into IP addresses that computers can understand. It's a decentralized system, with multiple DNS servers and providers worldwide.
what_you_will_learn: DNS resolution — understanding how DNS resolves domain names to IP addresses, including recursive and iterative queries
DNS record types — learning about the different types of DNS records (A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, SOA, TXT) and their purposes
DNS zone files — managing DNS zone files for a domain, including editing and updating records
DNS propagation — understanding how changes to DNS records propagate through the internet, including TTLs and caching
DNS security best practices — learning about common DNS-related security threats (e.g. DNS spoofing, cache poisoning) and mitigation strategies
DNSSEC (Domain Name System Security Extensions) — understanding how DNSSEC adds an additional layer of security to DNS by signing records with digital signatures

topics.section_name: What is and how to setup X ?

knowledge.what_it_is: Apache Tomcat is an open-source, widely-used web server and servlet container that supports Java-based web applications. It provides a flexible and scalable platform for deploying and managing web applications.
what_you_will_learn: Tomcat Configuration — understanding the configuration files (server.xml, context.xml) and their impact on application deployment
War File Deployment — deploying web applications as WAR files to Tomcat's webapps directory
Context Path Management — configuring context paths for multiple web applications running on a single Tomcat instance
SSL/TLS Configuration — setting up SSL/TLS certificates for secure communication between clients and Tomcat
Tomcat Manager App — using the Tomcat manager application to deploy, start, stop, and undeploy web applications
Monitoring and Logging — configuring Tomcat's logging and monitoring capabilities (e.g., access logs, error logs)
Security Considerations — understanding Tomcat's security features (e.g., authentication, authorization, access control)
Tomcat Clustering — configuring Tomcat for load balancing and high availability using clustering
Tomcat Upgrade/Downgrade — planning and executing upgrades or downgrades of Tomcat versions

topics.section_name: What is and how to setup X ?

knowledge.what_it_is: Apache is an open-source, cross-platform web server software that provides a flexible and scalable solution for serving HTTP requests. It is widely used in DevSecOps environments due to its ability to handle high traffic, provide load balancing, and integrate with various security features.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring Apache Virtual Hosts — setting up multiple websites on a single server using virtual hosts
Apache Modules — understanding the different modules available for Apache (e.g., mod_rewrite, mod_ssl) and how to configure them
SSL/TLS Configuration — setting up SSL certificates and configuring TLS encryption for secure communication
Load Balancing with Apache — using mod_proxy or mod_jk to distribute traffic across multiple servers
Apache Security Features — understanding and configuring features like IP blocking, password protection, and access control lists
Monitoring Apache Performance — using tools like Apache Bench (ab) and mod_status to monitor server performance and identify bottlenecks

topics.section_name: What is and how to setup X ?

knowledge.what_it_is: HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is a request-response protocol used for data communication between clients and servers on the web, enabling web browsers to interact with web servers. It's a crucial component of DevSecOps, ensuring secure and efficient data exchange.
what_you_will_learn: Request Methods — understanding HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) for different operations
HTTP Status Codes — learning about standard status codes (200-299, 300-399, 400-499, 500-599) and their meanings
HTTP Headers — mastering common headers (Host, Accept, Accept-Language, Content-Type, Authorization) and their usage
Cookies vs. Sessions — understanding the difference between cookies and sessions for user authentication and tracking
HTTP Caching — learning about cache control mechanisms (Cache-Control, ETag, Last-Modified) and caching strategies
HTTPS vs. HTTP — understanding the importance of HTTPS encryption and its implementation using SSL/TLS certificates

topics.section_name: What is and how to setup X ?

knowledge.what_it_is: HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) is an extension of HTTP that adds encryption and authentication between a client and server, ensuring secure data transfer over the internet. It is a crucial component of DevSecOps, ensuring confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of data.
what_you_will_learn: Certificate Authority (CA) — trusted third-party organizations issuing digital certificates for HTTPS
Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) — hierarchical system managing public-private key pairs for secure authentication
SSL/TLS handshake — process establishing a secure connection between client and server, including certificate verification and key exchange
HTTPS protocol layers — understanding the OSI model and TCP/IP stack in relation to HTTPS communication
Certificate validation — verifying certificate authenticity, checking revocation status, and ensuring trust chain integrity
Cipher suites — selecting suitable encryption algorithms for secure data transfer, balancing performance and security
Server-side configuration — setting up HTTPS on web servers like Nginx, Apache, or IIS, including SSL/TLS configuration files
Client-side configuration — configuring browsers to support HTTPS, including certificate pinning and trust store management

topics.section_name: What is and how to setup X ?

knowledge.what_it_is: IIS (Internet Information Services) is a web server software developed by Microsoft, used to host and manage websites, web applications, and services. It provides features such as URL rewriting, caching, compression, and SSL/TLS encryption.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring IIS sites — creating and managing website bindings, configuring IP addresses, and setting up virtual directories
IIS application pools — understanding worker processes, recycling, and configuration options for improved performance and reliability
URL rewriting with IIS URL Rewrite module — using regular expressions to manipulate URLs, redirecting requests, and caching frequently accessed resources
IIS caching — configuring output caching, fragment caching, and disk-based caching for improved page load times
SSL/TLS encryption with IIS — importing certificates, binding SSL/TLS to sites, and configuring secure protocols (HTTPS)
IIS logging and monitoring — setting up log files, configuring performance counters, and using built-in monitoring tools (Performance Monitor, Event Viewer)
IIS security features — understanding authentication methods (Windows Authentication, Forms-Based Authentication), authorization rules, and access control lists
Deploying IIS sites with Web Deploy — packaging and deploying websites, managing database connections, and handling configuration changes

topics.section_name: What is and how to setup X ?

knowledge.what_it_is: A caching server is a dedicated server that stores frequently accessed data or web pages to improve application performance and reduce latency, often used in DevSecOps environments to accelerate software delivery pipelines.
what_you_will_learn: Cache types — understanding the difference between memory-based caches (e.g., Redis), disk-based caches (e.g., Memcached), and hybrid caches (e.g., Hazelcast)
Cache invalidation strategies — learning about time-to-live (TTL) expiration, least recently used (LRU) eviction, and most frequently used (MFU) replacement policies
Cache clustering and replication — setting up distributed caching architectures using tools like Redis Cluster or Hazelcast
Cache configuration and management — understanding how to configure cache settings, monitor cache performance, and troubleshoot common issues with tools like Prometheus and Grafana
Integration with CDNs (Content Delivery Networks) — learning about the benefits of combining caching servers with CDNs for global content delivery and edge computing
Security considerations — discussing the importance of securing caching servers against data breaches and ensuring compliance with regulatory requirements

topics.section_name: What is and how to setup X ?

knowledge.what_it_is: A load balancer is a network device that distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers to improve responsiveness, reliability, and scalability of applications. In DevSecOps, load balancers are critical for ensuring high availability and fault tolerance.
what_you_will_learn: HAProxy — a popular open-source load balancing solution with advanced features like SSL termination and caching
NGINX — a web server and reverse proxy that can also act as a load balancer, known for its high performance and scalability
Pound — a reverse proxy and load balancer that supports multiple protocols, including HTTP, HTTPS, and FTP
Load balancing algorithms — understanding the differences between round-robin, least connection, IP hash, and session persistence
Session persistence — techniques for maintaining user sessions across multiple requests, such as cookie-based or source IP hashing
SSL termination — offloading SSL encryption from application servers to load balancers for improved performance and security
Health checks — configuring periodic checks on backend servers to ensure they are available and responding correctly

knowledge.what_it_is: SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) and TLS (Transport Layer Security) are cryptographic protocols used to secure online communication between a web browser and a server, ensuring confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of data. In DevSecOps, SSL/TLS is crucial for encrypting sensitive data in transit.
what_you_will_learn: SSL/TLS handshake process — understanding the exchange of keys and certificates during connection establishment
Certificate Authority (CA) hierarchy — knowing how root CAs, intermediate CAs, and leaf certificates work together to establish trust
Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) — managing public-private key pairs for secure authentication and encryption
SSL/TLS protocol versions — understanding the differences between SSLv2, SSLv3, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1, TLS 1.2, and TLS 1.3
Certificate validation — checking certificate revocation lists (CRLs), online certificate status protocol (OCSP) responses, and subject alternative names (SANs)
Key exchange algorithms — understanding the differences between RSA, Diffie-Hellman (DH), Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH), and elliptic curve cryptography (ECC)
Cipher suites — selecting suitable cipher suites for optimal performance and security
SSL/TLS pinning — hardening against SSL stripping attacks by specifying allowed certificate hashes or fingerprints

knowledge.what_it_is: Secure Shell (SSH) is a cryptographic network protocol for secure remote access to and management of Linux/Unix systems, created by Tatu Ylönen. It provides a secure way to execute commands on a remote server, transfer files, and manage system configurations.
what_you_will_learn: Key-based authentication — using public-private key pairs for secure login without passwords
SSH keys — generating, managing, and securing SSH keys for identity verification
SSH agents — configuring and using SSH agent to cache and reuse private keys
Port forwarding — securely tunneling network traffic through an SSH connection
Tunneling protocols — understanding SSH tunneling protocols (TCP/IP, UDP, ICMP) and their applications
SCP/SFTP — transferring files securely over SSH using SCP or SFTP protocols
SSH configuration files — managing and securing SSH configuration files on the server-side
SSH access control — configuring and implementing access controls for SSH connections

knowledge.what_it_is: The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model is a conceptual framework used to understand and analyze network communication protocols, consisting of seven layers: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, and Application. It's essential for DevSecOps engineers to comprehend the OSI model to troubleshoot and optimize network performance.
what_you_will_learn: Layer 1 (Physical) — Understanding physical layer protocols such as Ethernet, Wi-Fi, and Fiber Optics
Layer 2 (Data Link) — Familiarity with data link layer protocols like ARP, RARP, NDP, and LLDP for network discovery and configuration
Layer 3 (Network) — Knowledge of network layer protocols including IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, IGMP, and BGP for routing and addressing
Layer 4 (Transport) — Understanding transport layer protocols such as TCP, UDP, SCTP, and DCCP for reliable data transfer
Layer 5 (Session) — Familiarity with session layer protocols like NetBIOS, SSH, and Telnet for establishing and managing connections
Layer 6 (Presentation) — Knowledge of presentation layer protocols including SSL/TLS, PPTP, and L2TP for secure data transmission
Layer 7 (Application) — Understanding application layer protocols such as HTTP, FTP, SMTP, and DNS for communication between applications

knowledge.what_it_is: A forward proxy server sits between a client and a target server, forwarding requests from the client to the target server while modifying or manipulating the request data. In DevSecOps, forward proxies are used for security, caching, and content filtering.
what_you_will_learn: Proxy protocols — HTTP/1.1, SOCKS4, SOCKS5: understanding the differences in proxy protocol usage
Forward proxy configuration files — Apache, Nginx, HAProxy: learning how to configure forward proxies using popular web servers and load balancers
IP address spoofing prevention — IPVS, IPTables: understanding how to prevent IP spoofing attacks on forward proxies
SSL/TLS termination — NGINX, HAProxy, OpenSSL: learning how to terminate SSL/TLS connections at the forward proxy layer
Request filtering and modification — mod_security, OWASP CRS: understanding how to filter and modify request data using popular security modules
Authentication and authorization — LDAP, Active Directory, Kerberos: learning how to authenticate and authorize users accessing resources through a forward proxy
Logging and monitoring — ELK Stack, Splunk, Grafana: understanding how to log and monitor forward proxy activity for security and performance insights

knowledge.what_it_is: A reverse proxy server sits between clients and servers, forwarding requests from clients to servers while hiding the server's IP address and providing additional security features such as SSL termination, load balancing, and caching. Reverse proxies are commonly used in DevSecOps environments to improve application security, performance, and scalability.
what_you_will_learn: NGINX — a popular open-source reverse proxy server that supports HTTP/2, WebSockets, and SSL/TLS termination
HAProxy — a high-performance load balancer and reverse proxy server that supports TCP and UDP traffic
Apache HTTP Server with mod_proxy — a widely-used reverse proxy module for the Apache web server
Reverse Proxy Cache — using caching mechanisms to improve application performance by reducing the number of requests made to the origin server
SSL/TLS Termination — offloading SSL/TLS encryption from the origin server to the reverse proxy server
Load Balancing — distributing incoming traffic across multiple origin servers to improve responsiveness and availability
IP Address Hiding — concealing the origin server's IP address from clients using a reverse proxy
Content Compression — compressing responses from the origin server to reduce bandwidth usage and improve page load times

knowledge.what_it_is: A firewall is a network security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing traffic based on predetermined security rules, preventing unauthorized access to or from a private network.
what_you_will_learn: Firewall types — understanding the differences between network-based firewalls (e.g., Cisco ASA), host-based firewalls (e.g., Windows Defender Firewall), and application-level gateways (ALGs)
Rule-based filtering — creating and managing firewall rules to allow or block specific traffic based on source IP, destination IP, protocol, port, and other criteria
Stateful packet inspection (SPI) — understanding how firewalls track the state of network connections to prevent SYN flooding attacks and ensure proper connection teardown
Deep packet inspection (DPI) — learning about advanced firewall capabilities that inspect application-layer data for malware, DDoS protection, and intrusion prevention systems
Firewall configuration management — using tools like Ansible, Terraform, or Puppet to automate and version-control firewall configurations across multiple environments
Logging and monitoring — setting up log collection and analysis tools (e.g., ELK Stack, Splunk) to monitor firewall activity and detect potential security incidents

knowledge.what_it_is: Cloud providers offer scalable, on-demand computing resources over the internet, enabling organizations to build and deploy applications without managing underlying infrastructure.
what_you_will_learn: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) — provisioning virtual machines, storage, and networking resources from AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, or other cloud providers
PaaS (Platform as a Service) — deploying applications on managed platforms like Heroku, Vercel, or Netlify, which handle infrastructure and middleware
SaaS (Software as a Service) — using pre-built software applications like Salesforce, Microsoft 365, or Google Workspace, without requiring local installation or maintenance
Cloud provider selection criteria — evaluating factors such as cost, scalability, security, compliance, and support for DevSecOps practices
Cloud migration strategies — planning and executing the transition of existing applications to cloud environments, including data transfer and application refactoring
Cloud security best practices — implementing measures like encryption, access controls, monitoring, and incident response to protect cloud-based resources
Cloud cost optimization techniques — using tools like AWS Cost Explorer, Azure Cost Estimator, or Google Cloud Cost Management to minimize waste and reduce expenses

knowledge.what_it_is: AWS (Amazon Web Services) is a comprehensive cloud platform that provides a wide range of services and tools for building, deploying, and managing applications and infrastructure. It offers a pay-as-you-go pricing model, scalability, reliability, and security features.
what_you_will_learn: IAM roles — managing access to AWS resources using identity and access management policies
EC2 instances — creating and configuring virtual machines in the cloud for application hosting
S3 buckets — storing and serving static website content, images, videos, and other files
CloudFormation templates — defining infrastructure as code (IaC) using YAML or JSON files
CodePipeline — automating continuous integration and delivery (CI/CD) of applications using AWS services
CloudWatch logs — monitoring and logging application performance, errors, and security events in real-time
Route53 DNS — managing domain names, routing traffic to AWS resources, and implementing failover strategies
EBS volumes — attaching persistent storage to EC2 instances for data persistence

knowledge.what_it_is: Azure is Microsoft's cloud computing platform, offering a wide range of services for building, deploying, and managing applications and infrastructure. It provides a highly available, scalable, and secure environment for DevSecOps teams to work with.
what_you_will_learn: Resource Groups — organizing Azure resources into logical groups for easier management
Virtual Machines (VMs) — creating and configuring Windows or Linux VMs for application hosting
Azure Storage — managing data with Blob, File, Queue, and Table storage services
Azure Networking — configuring virtual networks, subnets, and network security groups
Azure Active Directory (AAD) — integrating identity and access management for applications
Azure DevOps Services — using Azure Pipelines for CI/CD, Azure Repos for version control, and Azure Boards for project planning
Azure Monitor — collecting and analyzing logs and metrics for application performance monitoring

knowledge.what_it_is: Google Cloud is a suite of cloud computing services offered by Google, providing scalable infrastructure and tools for building, deploying, and managing applications. It includes services like Compute Engine, Kubernetes Engine, App Engine, Cloud Storage, and more.
what_you_will_learn: Compute Engine — creating virtual machines (VMs) with custom configurations, using instance groups and managed instances
Kubernetes Engine — deploying containerized applications to a managed Kubernetes cluster, using node pools and persistent disks
Cloud Storage — storing and serving large files and datasets, using buckets, objects, and lifecycle management
Cloud IAM — managing access control and identity across Google Cloud resources, using roles, permissions, and service accounts
Cloud Logging and Monitoring — collecting and analyzing logs and metrics from applications and infrastructure, using Log Explorer and Monitoring
Cloud Security Command Center (CSCC) — detecting and responding to security threats in real-time, using threat intelligence and incident response
Cloud Functions — building event-driven serverless applications, using Node.js, Python, or Go runtime environments
Cloud Build — automating the build, test, and deployment of applications across multiple platforms, using pipelines and triggers

knowledge.what_it_is: DigitalOcean is a cloud platform that provides scalable, on-demand infrastructure and services to build, deploy, and manage applications. It offers a range of products including virtual machines, block storage, load balancers, and firewalls.
what_you_will_learn: Creating Droplets — DigitalOcean's term for virtual machines, which can be customized with various operating systems, CPU, memory, and storage configurations
Configuring Networking — setting up private networks, public IP addresses, and port forwarding for secure communication between Droplets
Managing Storage — using block storage volumes to persist data across reboots, snapshots for backups, and object storage for large-scale data storage
Implementing Security Groups — defining firewall rules to control incoming and outgoing traffic, and setting up load balancers for high availability
Deploying Applications — using DigitalOcean's 1-Click Apps to deploy popular applications like WordPress, Laravel, and Django with a single click
Monitoring and Logging — using DigitalOcean's built-in monitoring and logging tools to track resource usage, performance metrics, and error logs

knowledge.what_it_is: Cloudflare is a cloud-based security, performance, and content delivery network (CDN) platform that helps protect and accelerate websites, applications, and APIs. It provides a suite of tools to secure web applications from common web vulnerabilities, DDoS attacks, and malware.
what_you_will_learn: Cloudflare DNS — configuring Cloudflare as a DNS provider for domain name resolution
SSL/TLS certificates — managing SSL/TLS certificates with Cloudflare's built-in certificate manager
IP blocking — blocking IP addresses at the network level to prevent DDoS attacks
Rate limiting — controlling traffic rates to prevent abuse and DDoS attacks
Web Application Firewall (WAF) — configuring WAF rules to protect against common web vulnerabilities
Bot Management — identifying and mitigating bot traffic to prevent scraping and abuse
Cloudflare Zero Trust — implementing zero-trust security policies for secure access to applications
Armed DDoS Protection — detecting and mitigating advanced DDoS attacks with AI-powered protection

knowledge.what_it_is: Alibaba Cloud is a comprehensive cloud computing platform that provides a wide range of services, including compute, storage, networking, and security. It is designed to support DevSecOps workflows with features like automated deployment, continuous integration and delivery, and real-time monitoring.
what_you_will_learn: CloudFormation — defining infrastructure as code using JSON or YAML templates
Resource Groups — organizing resources into logical groups for better management and security
Security Groups — controlling inbound and outbound traffic to instances based on protocols and ports
RAM Roles — managing access and permissions for users and services across Alibaba Cloud accounts
CloudWatch Logs — monitoring and analyzing log data from applications and services in real-time
Serverless Compute — running code without provisioning or managing servers, using functions like Function Compute
Container Service for EC2 (CSE) — deploying and managing containerized applications at scale
Alibaba Cloud Security Center — providing a unified security management platform for threat detection and response

knowledge.what_it_is: Hetzner is a German cloud hosting provider offering scalable infrastructure solutions, including dedicated servers, virtual private servers (VPS), and cloud computing services. It provides a range of tools and APIs for automating deployment, scaling, and management of applications.
what_you_will_learn: Server provisioning — creating and managing Hetzner servers using the Hetzner API or web interface
IP address management — assigning and managing IP addresses for Hetzner servers
Network configuration — configuring network settings, including firewall rules and routing tables
Storage management — managing storage resources, including disk space allocation and snapshotting
Load balancing — distributing traffic across multiple Hetzner servers using load balancers like HAProxy or NGINX
Monitoring and logging — setting up monitoring tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and log aggregation with ELK Stack
Security best practices — implementing security measures like firewall rules, SSH keys, and access control lists (ACLs)
Automation with Terraform — using HashiCorp's Terraform to automate Hetzner infrastructure provisioning and management

knowledge.what_it_is: Contabo is a German cloud hosting provider offering scalable, secure, and cost-effective infrastructure solutions for DevSecOps teams. It provides a range of services including virtual servers, storage, networking, and load balancing.
what_you_will_learn: Creating and managing virtual servers in Contabo using the web console or API — configuring CPU, RAM, storage, and network settings
Setting up and managing block storage volumes for persistent data storage — attaching to virtual servers, snapshotting, and cloning
Configuring networking options including public IP addresses, private networks, and load balancing — ensuring high availability and scalability
Implementing security measures such as firewalls, access controls, and SSL/TLS certificates — protecting against unauthorized access and data breaches
Using Contabo's API to automate infrastructure provisioning and management through scripts and tools like Ansible or Terraform
Monitoring and optimizing resource utilization using Contabo's built-in metrics and logging features — ensuring efficient use of resources and cost optimization

knowledge.what_it_is: Heroku is a cloud platform as a service (PaaS) that allows developers to build, deploy, and manage applications without worrying about the underlying infrastructure. It supports a wide range of programming languages, including Node.js, Python, Java, and Ruby.
what_you_will_learn: Dynos — isolated units of memory and CPU resources for running applications
Heroku Postgres — managed PostgreSQL database service with automatic backups and scaling
Heroku Redis — in-memory data store that persists to disk, supporting pub/sub messaging and transactions
Procfile — configuration file specifying how to run the application on Heroku (e.g., web: node server.js)
Slug — pre-built image of the application environment, including dependencies and configuration
Heroku CLI — command-line interface for interacting with Heroku resources, such as apps, dynos, and add-ons
Git-based deployment — using Git to manage code changes and deploy to Heroku
Config Vars — key-value pairs stored in the app's environment that can be accessed by the application

knowledge.what_it_is: Vercel is a cloud platform that provides a suite of tools for building, deploying, and managing modern web applications, with a focus on speed, security, and scalability. It supports popular frameworks like Next.js, React, and Vue.js, and integrates with popular version control systems like GitHub and GitLab.
what_you_will_learn: Vercel CLI — using the command-line interface to deploy and manage applications
Vercel Platform — understanding the core features of Vercel's platform, including edge network, caching, and content delivery
Edge Network — leveraging Vercel's global network for faster content delivery and reduced latency
Serverless Functions — deploying serverless functions using Vercel's built-in support for AWS Lambda and Google Cloud Functions
Static Site Generation (SSG) — optimizing application performance with static site generation using frameworks like Next.js
CDN Integration — integrating Vercel's content delivery network (CDN) with popular CDNs like Cloudflare and MaxCDN
Security Features — understanding Vercel's security features, including SSL/TLS encryption, DDoS protection, and IP blocking
Monitoring and Analytics — using Vercel's built-in monitoring and analytics tools to track application performance and user behavior

knowledge.what_it_is: Netlify is a cloud-based platform that provides a suite of tools and services for building, deploying, and managing modern web applications, with a focus on DevSecOps practices such as automated security scanning, continuous integration/continuous deployment (CI/CD), and serverless architecture.
what_you_will_learn: Netlify CMS — a headless CMS that integrates with Netlify to provide a decoupled content management experience
Netlify Functions — a serverless function platform for building API endpoints, event handlers, and other backend logic
Netlify Edge — a global network of edge servers that cache and serve static assets, reducing latency and improving performance
Netlify Identity — a user authentication and authorization service that integrates with Netlify CMS and other services
Automated security scanning — using tools like Snyk or OWASP ZAP to scan for vulnerabilities in dependencies and code
CI/CD pipelines — setting up automated build, test, and deployment workflows using Netlify's built-in pipeline feature or third-party tools like Jenkins or CircleCI
Serverless architecture — designing applications that use serverless functions, edge computing, and other cloud-native services to reduce costs and improve scalability

knowledge.what_it_is: AWS Lambda is a serverless compute service provided by AWS, allowing developers to run code without provisioning or managing servers. It's event-driven, scalable, and highly available, making it an ideal choice for DevSecOps teams.
what_you_will_learn: Function triggers — understanding how AWS Lambda functions are triggered by events such as API Gateway requests, S3 object uploads, or DynamoDB updates
Runtime environments — choosing from Node.js, Python, Go, Ruby, and .NET Core for executing Lambda functions
Memory and execution time limits — managing function memory allocation (up to 3008 MB) and execution time (up to 15 minutes)
Event source mappings — configuring AWS services like S3, DynamoDB, or Kinesis as event sources for Lambda functions
Function versions and aliases — versioning and aliasing Lambda functions for easier management and deployment
Dead-letter queues — setting up error handling mechanisms for failed function executions
X-Ray integration — using AWS X-Ray to monitor and debug Lambda functions at scale
AWS IAM roles and permissions — securing access to AWS services and resources from within Lambda functions

knowledge.what_it_is: Azure Functions is a fully managed, event-driven serverless compute service provided by Microsoft Azure, allowing developers to build scalable and highly available applications without worrying about underlying infrastructure.
what_you_will_learn: Trigger types — HTTP, Timer, Queue, Service Bus, Blob Storage, Event Grid: understanding the different types of triggers that can be used in Azure Functions
Bindings — Cosmos DB, SQL Database, Table Storage, Azure Blob Storage, Azure Queue Storage: learning how to integrate Azure Functions with various Azure services using bindings
Function runtime versions — .NET Core 3.1, Node.js 14, Python 3.9: understanding the different function runtime versions available and their implications on code compatibility
Azure Functions v2 vs v3 — differences in configuration, deployment, and scaling: learning about the key differences between Azure Functions v2 and v3
Proxies and routes — configuring custom domains, routing rules, and proxying HTTP requests: understanding how to configure proxies and routes in Azure Functions
Durable Task Framework (DTF) — building long-running workflows with state management, retries, and timeouts: learning about the DTF and its use cases
Azure Storage Queues and Service Bus — integrating with message queues for decoupling and scalability: understanding how to integrate Azure Functions with message queues

knowledge.what_it_is: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) Functions is a serverless compute service that allows developers to run small code snippets or functions in response to events, without worrying about the underlying infrastructure. It's designed for event-driven programming and supports various triggers such as HTTP requests, Cloud Storage changes, and Pub/Sub messages.
what_you_will_learn: Trigger types — understanding the different types of triggers supported by GCP Functions, including HTTP, Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, and more
Function execution environment — learning about the isolated environment in which functions run, including access to dependencies and services like Cloud SQL and Cloud Storage
Event-driven programming model — mastering the event-driven paradigm for building scalable and efficient serverless applications
Cloud Events format — understanding how GCP Functions uses the Cloud Events format to standardize event payloads and make them easily consumable by functions
Function deployment options — learning about the various ways to deploy functions, including direct upload, Cloud Source Repositories, and Cloud Build
Security and authentication — configuring security settings for functions, including IAM roles, service accounts, and encryption at rest and in transit
Error handling and logging — understanding how to handle errors and log events in GCP Functions using the Stackdriver Logging and Monitoring services

knowledge.what_it_is: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is a protocol used to send and receive email messages between mail servers, ensuring secure and reliable communication over the internet. In DevSecOps, understanding SMTP is crucial for implementing robust email security measures.
what_you_will_learn: Mail server configuration — setting up Postfix or Exim as SMTP relay hosts
TLS encryption — configuring SSL/TLS certificates for mail servers to encrypt email traffic
Authentication mechanisms — implementing SPF, DKIM, and DMARC to prevent spoofing and phishing attacks
Email filtering — using SpamAssassin or other tools to filter out spam and malicious emails
Mail server hardening — securing mail servers against exploits and vulnerabilities
SMTP relay configuration — setting up SMTP relays for load balancing and high availability
Email logging and monitoring — implementing tools like Logwatch or Splunk to monitor email activity

knowledge.what_it_is: DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance) is an email validation system designed to prevent email spoofing and phishing. It's a security protocol that helps domain owners verify the authenticity of emails sent from their domains.
what_you_will_learn: DMARC policies — configuring DMARC records in DNS to specify authentication requirements for incoming emails
Authentication algorithms — understanding how DMARC uses SPF, DKIM, and Sender Policy Framework (SPF) to authenticate email senders
Reporting mechanisms — setting up reporting tools like Google's DMARC Reporting Tool or Cloudmark's DMARC Analyzer to monitor and analyze DMARC data
Alignment modes — configuring DMARC policies to specify how to handle emails that fail authentication checks, including p=none, p=quarantine, and p=reject
DMARC record syntax — understanding the format of DMARC records in DNS, including the 'v=' version number, 'p=' policy, and 'rua=' reporting URI
SPF integration — setting up SPF to work with DMARC, including configuring SPF records in DNS and verifying SPF results in DMARC reports
DKIM integration — understanding how DKIM works with DMARC, including configuring DKIM keys and signing emails with DKIM
DMARC policy tags — using specific tags like 'fo' (Feedback-Forwarding) and 'pct' (Percentage) to customize DMARC policies

knowledge.what_it_is: IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol) is a protocol used for accessing email on remote servers, allowing users to read, send, and manage emails. It's widely used in DevSecOps environments for secure email communication.
what_you_will_learn: Authentication mechanisms — IMAP supports various authentication methods, including PLAIN, LOGIN, CRAM-MD5, and NTLM
Mailbox management — creating, deleting, and managing mailboxes on the server using IMAP commands like CREATE, DELETE, and RENAME
Message retrieval — fetching emails from the server using IMAP commands like FETCH, STORE, and EXPUNGE
Folder synchronization — synchronizing email folders between the client and server using IMAP's folder management features
Security considerations — understanding IMAP security risks, such as password sniffing and man-in-the-middle attacks, and implementing countermeasures like SSL/TLS encryption
IMAP capabilities — understanding IMAP capabilities, including support for extensions like IDLE, QRESYNC, and ESEARCH
IMAP client configuration — configuring IMAP clients to connect securely to the server using settings like port numbers, authentication mechanisms, and encryption protocols

knowledge.what_it_is: Sender Policy Framework (SPF) is an email authentication protocol that helps prevent spam and phishing by verifying the sender's IP address. It is a DNS record type that specifies which IP addresses are authorized to send emails on behalf of a domain.
what_you_will_learn: SPF records — creating and managing SPF records in DNS, including 'include' and 'ip4' mechanisms
SPF syntax — understanding the format and structure of SPF records, including 'a', 'mx', and 'ip4' specifiers
SPF policies — defining and implementing SPF policies for different scenarios, such as allowing or blocking specific IP addresses
SPF mechanisms — learning about common SPF mechanisms, including 'include', 'ip4', and 'exists'
SPF record types — understanding the different types of SPF records, including 'TXT' and 'PTR'
SPF validation — how to validate SPF records using tools like 'dig' or online SPF validators
SPF best practices — learning about common mistakes and pitfalls when implementing SPF, such as incorrect syntax or missing mechanisms

knowledge.what_it_is: POP3S (Post Office Protocol version 3 over SSL/TLS) is a secure email protocol used to retrieve and manage email messages from mail servers. It provides encryption and authentication, ensuring secure communication between clients and servers.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring POP3S on mail servers — enabling SSL/TLS certificates for encrypted connections
POP3S client configuration — setting up email clients like Outlook or Thunderbird to connect via POP3S
Email encryption methods — understanding the differences between TLS, SSL, and PGP encryption in POP3S
Authentication mechanisms — learning about SASL (Simple Authentication and Security Layer) and PLAIN authentication for POP3S
POP3S port configuration — setting up the correct port numbers (995 or 465) for POP3S connections
Email client security settings — configuring email clients to use secure protocols like POP3S and IMAP over SSL/TLS

knowledge.what_it_is: Domain Keys (DKIM, DMARC, SPF) are email authentication protocols that help prevent spam and phishing by verifying sender identity and domain ownership.
what_you_will_learn: DKIM — DomainKeys Identified Mail: using public-private key pairs to sign emails and verify authenticity
DMARC — Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance: enforcing policies for DKIM and SPF, reporting on authentication results
SPF — Sender Policy Framework: specifying which IP addresses are authorized to send emails on behalf of a domain
DKIM signing process — generating public-private key pairs, configuring DNS records, and adding signatures to outgoing emails
DMARC record configuration — setting policies for DKIM and SPF, defining reporting mechanisms, and handling authentication failures
SPF record configuration — specifying authorized IP addresses, networks, or services for sending emails on behalf of a domain
Email authentication best practices — implementing Domain Keys, DMARC, and SPF in conjunction with other security measures
Common email authentication pitfalls — misconfigured DNS records, incorrect DKIM/DMARC policies, and SPF record errors
subtopics: domain-based message authentication reporting and conformance

knowledge.what_it_is: White listing and grey listing are email authentication techniques used to prevent spam and phishing attacks by allowing or blocking emails based on sender reputation, IP address, and domain validation.
what_you_will_learn: Sender Policy Framework (SPF) — a DNS record that specifies which IP addresses are authorized to send emails on behalf of your domain
DomainKeys Identified Mail (DKIM) — a digital signature that verifies the authenticity of an email by checking its cryptographic signature
Sender ID Framework (SIDF) — a protocol for authenticating email senders and preventing spoofing attacks
Grey listing — a technique where incoming emails are temporarily blocked or delayed to determine their legitimacy before allowing them through
White listing — a technique where trusted senders are automatically allowed through without further verification
Authentication-Results (AR) header — a header that provides information about the authentication results of an email, including SPF, DKIM, and DMARC checks
DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance) — a protocol for enforcing policies on incoming emails based on their authentication status

knowledge.what_it_is: Provisioning is the process of setting up and configuring IT resources, such as servers, networks, and storage, to support DevSecOps pipelines. It involves automating the deployment of infrastructure and applications to reduce manual errors and increase efficiency.
what_you_will_learn: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools — using Terraform, AWS CloudFormation, or Azure Resource Manager to define and manage cloud infrastructure
Containerization — using Docker to package and deploy applications in containers for consistent and portable environments
Kubernetes — deploying and managing containerized applications at scale with orchestration and automation
Cloud Provider APIs — interacting with AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, or other cloud providers through their respective APIs for infrastructure management
Ansible or Puppet — using configuration management tools to automate the deployment of software configurations across multiple servers
Provisioning templates — creating reusable templates in Terraform or other IaC tools to streamline infrastructure setup and reduce errors
Dynamic inventory — managing dynamic inventories with Ansible or other automation tools for efficient resource allocation
Role-based access control (RBAC) — implementing RBAC policies to manage user permissions and access to provisioned resources

knowledge.what_it_is: Ansible is an open-source automation tool used for configuration management, deployment, and orchestration of infrastructure as code (IaC). It uses a human-readable YAML or Ansible Playbook language to define automation tasks.
what_you_will_learn: Playbooks — defining automation tasks in YAML or Ansible Playbook language
Roles — reusable collections of playbooks, templates, and variables for consistent infrastructure deployment
Inventory — managing host groups and dynamic inventory with tools like AWX or Ansible Tower
Modules — pre-built Ansible code for common tasks like user management, package installation, and file transfer
Handlers — running tasks on remote hosts only when necessary, improving efficiency and reducing noise
Templates — using Jinja2 templating engine to generate dynamic configuration files from variables
Variables — defining and using variables across playbooks for consistency and reusability
Conditionals — using if-else statements to control playbook execution based on conditions

knowledge.what_it_is: Chef is an open-source infrastructure as code (IaC) tool that automates the configuration and management of servers, networks, and other IT resources. It uses a declarative syntax to define desired states, allowing for efficient and repeatable deployments.
what_you_will_learn: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — defining infrastructure configurations using code
Chef Recipes — writing cookbooks that contain recipes for automating tasks
Cookbooks — organizing related recipes into a single cookbook
Resources — declaring resources, such as files, directories, and packages
Providers — specifying the underlying infrastructure provider (e.g., AWS, Azure)
Attributes — defining attributes for nodes or roles to customize configurations
Roles — assigning responsibilities to nodes using role-based access control
Environments — defining environments for different deployment scenarios

knowledge.what_it_is: Puppet is an open-source configuration management tool that automates the deployment, configuration, and management of infrastructure resources such as servers, networks, and services. It uses a declarative syntax to define desired state, and Puppet's engine ensures that the actual state matches the declared state.
what_you_will_learn: Puppet Manifests — writing Puppet code in Ruby to define nodes, resources, and relationships
Classes and Modules — organizing Puppet code into reusable modules and classes for better maintainability
Resources and Providers — understanding how Puppet interacts with underlying systems using providers and resources
Nodes and Groups — defining and managing groups of nodes and their associated configurations
PuppetDB — storing and querying metadata about Puppet-managed nodes, including facts and resource state
Hiera and External Facts — using data externalization to manage configuration data at scale
Puppet Agent and Master Architecture — understanding the communication flow between Puppet agents and masters

knowledge.what_it_is: Jenkins is an open-source automation server that enables Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines, created by Kohsuke Kawaguchi. It supports a wide range of programming languages and tools, including Java, Python, Node.js, and more.
what_you_will_learn: Pipeline as Code — defining CI/CD workflows using Groovy scripts or Jenkinsfile in YAML format
Job Configuration — creating and managing jobs, including triggers, build steps, and post-build actions
Build Steps — executing shell commands, Maven builds, Gradle builds, and other tasks within a job
Declarative Pipelines — writing pipelines using a declarative syntax in Jenkinsfile, reducing errors and improving readability
Multibranch Pipelines — automating pipeline creation for multiple branches, including feature branches and release branches
Docker Support — integrating Docker containers into Jenkins jobs for automated testing and deployment
Security Scanning — integrating security tools like OWASP ZAP and Veracode to scan applications for vulnerabilities

knowledge.what_it_is: CircleCI is a leading continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) platform, used by top tech companies like Google, Amazon, and Microsoft. It automates testing, building, and deployment of software applications, ensuring faster time-to-market and improved quality.
what_you_will_learn: Config files — defining CircleCI workflows using YAML or JSON
Jobs — grouping related tasks into reusable units (e.g., build, test, deploy)
