Commands — executing shell commands within jobs (e.g., npm install, docker build)
Environment variables — storing and referencing sensitive data across workflows
Contexts — defining reusable sets of environment variables for specific use cases
Workflows — orchestrating multiple jobs into a single, cohesive process
Parameterization — injecting values from external sources (e.g., GitHub, CircleCI UI)
CircleCI orbs — pre-built, configurable packages for common tasks (e.g., deployment to AWS)

knowledge.what_it_is: GitLab CI/CD is a continuous integration and delivery tool that automates software builds, testing, and deployment within GitLab's DevOps platform. It integrates with GitLab's version control system to automate workflows and provide real-time feedback.
what_you_will_learn: CI/CD pipelines — defining automated workflows for building, testing, and deploying code using .gitlab-ci.yml files
Jobs and stages — breaking down complex workflows into smaller tasks and dependencies using job and stage definitions
Environment variables — managing sensitive data and secrets within CI/CD pipelines using predefined variables or encrypted values
Artifacts and caching — storing intermediate build results and dependencies to improve pipeline performance and reduce build times
Dependency management — automating package installations and version updates for dependent libraries and frameworks
GitLab Runner — installing and configuring the GitLab Runner service on-premises or in the cloud to execute CI/CD pipelines
CI/CD triggers — using webhooks, schedules, or manual triggers to initiate pipeline execution based on specific events or conditions

knowledge.what_it_is: A drone is an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) used for DevSecOps tasks such as infrastructure inspection, asset tracking, and security monitoring. In the context of DevSecOps, drones are used to automate and improve the efficiency of security-related tasks.
what_you_will_learn: Drone programming languages — including Python, C++, and Java, used for drone development and operation
Drone frameworks — such as PX4, ArduPilot, and DroneKit, providing a structured approach to drone development
Sensor integration — learning about various sensors (e.g., cameras, lidar, GPS) and how to integrate them with drones
Autopilot systems — understanding the role of autopilot systems in stabilizing and controlling drone flight
Drone communication protocols — including MAVLink, UDP, and TCP/IP, used for data transmission between drones and ground control stations
Drone security considerations — learning about potential security risks associated with drone usage (e.g., hacking, unauthorized access)
Drone deployment strategies — understanding how to deploy drones in various environments (e.g., indoor, outdoor, industrial)
Drone data analysis — learning how to collect and analyze data from drones for DevSecOps tasks

knowledge.what_it_is: GitHub Actions is a CI/CD tool that automates software builds, tests, and deployments directly within GitHub repositories. It integrates with various services like AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, and more.
what_you_will_learn: Workflows — defining automated tasks using YAML files (.yml) or JSON files (.json)
Jobs — grouping related tasks together for execution (e.g., build, test, deploy)
Steps — individual actions within a job, such as running commands or scripts
Actions — reusable components that perform specific tasks, like building Docker images or deploying to Kubernetes
Environment variables — injecting secrets and configuration values into workflows
Conditional statements — using if/else logic to control workflow execution based on conditions
Error handling — catching and managing errors within workflows using try/catch blocks
Artifact storage — storing build artifacts, like compiled binaries or deployment packages

knowledge.what_it_is: TeamCity is a commercial, web-based continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) server developed by JetBrains, widely used in DevSecOps pipelines to automate build, test, and deployment processes.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring TeamCity agents — setting up remote runners for distributed builds and tests
Creating and managing build configurations — defining triggers, dependencies, and artifacts
Using VCS triggers — integrating with Git, SVN, Mercurial, and Perforce repositories for automated builds
Implementing feature branches and merge requests — using TeamCity to manage code reviews and approvals
Configuring and using the TeamCity build agent — installing and configuring agents on Windows, Linux, or macOS
Monitoring and reporting with TeamCity dashboards — tracking build success rates, test coverage, and deployment metrics

knowledge.what_it_is: Travis CI is a popular open-source continuous integration (CI) tool, used by DevSecOps teams to automate testing and deployment of software projects. It supports various programming languages, including Java, Python, Ruby, Node.js, and more.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring .travis.yml file — defining build environment, dependencies, and test commands
Understanding Travis CI jobs — creating separate stages for testing, deployment, and reporting
Using environment variables in Travis CI — injecting secrets, credentials, and configuration settings
Implementing automated testing with Travis CI — running unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests
Deploying to cloud platforms using Travis CI — integrating with AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, and more
Monitoring build results and notifications in Travis CI — setting up email alerts, Slack notifications, and webhooks

knowledge.what_it_is: AWS CDK (Cloud Development Kit) is an open-source framework that enables developers to define cloud infrastructure in code using familiar programming languages such as TypeScript, Python, and Java. It provides a declarative way to create and manage AWS resources, promoting infrastructure as code (IaC).
what_you_will_learn: CDK Constructs — building blocks for defining cloud infrastructure, including AWS services and resources
Stacks — grouping related resources together, enabling modular and reusable infrastructure definitions
Environments — defining environment-specific configurations, such as dev, prod, or staging
AWS Provider — integrating with AWS services, including S3, EC2, RDS, and more
CDK Apps — creating and managing CDK applications, including dependencies and imports
Synthetic Imports — importing AWS resources into your CDK application without explicit imports
Contextualization — using context to customize resource configurations based on environment or other factors

knowledge.what_it_is: CloudFormation is an AWS service that allows you to create and manage infrastructure as code (IaC) using a simple text file, known as a CloudFormation template. It provides a declarative way to define your cloud resources, such as EC2 instances, S3 buckets, and RDS databases, and can be used for both provisioning and configuration management.
what_you_will_learn: CloudFormation templates — writing YAML or JSON files that describe your infrastructure, using AWS-provided data types and functions
Resource types — understanding the different resource types available in CloudFormation, such as EC2Instance, S3Bucket, and RDSDBInstance
Properties and parameters — defining input values for resources, using properties and parameters to customize your infrastructure
Mappings and conditions — using mappings and conditions to simplify your templates and make them more reusable
Stacks and nested stacks — creating complex infrastructure topologies by nesting stacks within each other
Change sets and drift detection — understanding how CloudFormation tracks changes to your infrastructure and detects drift between the desired state and actual state

knowledge.what_it_is: Pulumi is an infrastructure as code (IaC) platform that allows DevSecOps engineers to define, deploy, and manage cloud-native applications using programming languages like TypeScript, Python, or Go. It supports a wide range of cloud providers, including AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, and Kubernetes.
what_you_will_learn: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — defining infrastructure resources in code for version control and reproducibility
Pulumi Programs — writing Pulumi code using TypeScript, Python, or Go to define cloud infrastructure
Resource Providers — utilizing provider SDKs to interact with cloud providers' APIs and manage resources
Stacks — organizing related resources into logical groups for easier management and deployment
Secret Management — securely storing and managing sensitive data like API keys and credentials using Pulumi's built-in secrets management
Policy as Code (PaC) — defining security policies in code to enforce compliance and governance across cloud resources

knowledge.what_it_is: Terraform is an open-source infrastructure as code (IaC) tool developed by HashiCorp, used to manage and provision cloud and on-premises resources. It allows DevSecOps teams to define and maintain a consistent and reproducible infrastructure configuration across different environments.
what_you_will_learn: Providers — configuring Terraform to work with AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, or other supported platforms
Resources — defining cloud resources such as EC2 instances, S3 buckets, or RDS databases using HCL syntax
Modules — organizing and reusing Terraform configuration code for easier management and sharing
State Management — understanding how Terraform stores and manages state information across different environments
Workspaces — managing multiple infrastructure configurations and environments within a single Terraform setup
Input Variables — defining and using input variables to customize Terraform configurations
Output Values — extracting and exposing output values from Terraform resources for use in other tools or scripts
Terraform CLI — using the command-line interface to initialize, plan, apply, and destroy infrastructure configurations

knowledge.what_it_is: Sealed Secrets is an open-source tool that securely stores sensitive data like API keys, database credentials, and other secrets as Kubernetes ConfigMaps or Secret resources. It provides a centralized way to manage and rotate secrets across multiple environments.
what_you_will_learn: ConfigMap vs Secret — understanding the difference between these two Kubernetes resources for storing sensitive data
Sealed Secrets architecture — how it uses encryption, decryption, and verification to protect secrets in transit and at rest
Kubernetes ConfigMaps and Secrets integration — how Sealed Secrets integrates with existing Kubernetes workflows and tools like Helm and Kustomize
Secret rotation and renewal — automating the process of updating and rotating sensitive data using Sealed Secrets' built-in features
Encryption and decryption mechanisms — understanding the cryptographic algorithms and protocols used by Sealed Secrets to protect secrets
Verification and validation processes — how Sealed Secrets ensures the integrity and authenticity of encrypted secrets
Sealed Secrets CLI and API usage — hands-on experience with using the tool to manage and store sensitive data in Kubernetes environments

knowledge.what_it_is: Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) are documented, step-by-step guides that outline the tasks and activities required to achieve a specific goal or objective within DevSecOps. They ensure consistency, reduce errors, and improve efficiency across teams.
what_you_will_learn: Defining SOPs — creating clear, concise documents that outline tasks, responsibilities, and decision-making processes
Categorizing SOPs — grouping related procedures into categories (e.g., deployment, monitoring, security) for easy access and maintenance
Version control of SOPs — using tools like Git or SVN to track changes, collaborate with team members, and maintain a record of revisions
Automating SOPs — leveraging automation tools (e.g., Ansible, Terraform) to streamline repetitive tasks and reduce manual errors
SOP review and approval process — establishing a formal review and approval process to ensure SOPs are accurate, up-to-date, and aligned with organizational goals
Training and onboarding for SOPs — providing regular training sessions and documentation to help team members understand and follow SOPs
Monitoring and auditing SOPs — regularly reviewing and updating SOPs to reflect changes in technology, processes, or organizational policies

knowledge.what_it_is: HashiCorp Vault is a secrets management tool that securely stores and manages sensitive data such as API keys, database credentials, and encryption keys. It provides a centralized platform for DevSecOps teams to manage secrets across multiple environments and applications.
what_you_will_learn: Secrets Management — storing and retrieving sensitive data using Vault's dynamic secrets engine
Authentication Methods — configuring authentication methods such as token-based, username/password, and LDAP
Policies and Permissions — defining access control policies and permissions for users and teams
Mounting Secrets Engines — integrating with external services like AWS, GCP, and Azure to manage cloud-specific secrets
Encryption at Rest and in Transit — using Vault's encryption features to protect data both at rest and in transit
Vault Server Configuration — understanding the different configuration options for the Vault server, including HA and clustering
Client SDKs and APIs — using Vault's client SDKs and APIs to integrate with applications and services

knowledge.what_it_is: Infrastructure monitoring involves collecting and analyzing data from various IT infrastructure components to ensure they are running smoothly, efficiently, and securely. It's a critical aspect of DevSecOps, enabling teams to detect issues before they impact users and respond quickly to incidents.
what_you_will_learn: Metrics collection — understanding how to collect metrics from infrastructure components using tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and Datadog
Log aggregation — learning how to collect, process, and analyze logs from various sources using ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana), Splunk, or Sumo Logic
Alerting and notification — configuring alerting systems like PagerDuty, OpsGenie, or VictorOps to notify teams of potential issues
Infrastructure visualization — learning how to visualize infrastructure topology using tools like Graphite, Nagios, or Zabbix
Anomaly detection — understanding how to use machine learning algorithms to detect anomalies in infrastructure metrics and logs
Incident response — developing a plan for responding to incidents, including containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident activities
Capacity planning — learning how to analyze infrastructure usage patterns to ensure adequate capacity and prevent bottlenecks
Resource optimization — understanding how to optimize resource utilization using techniques like rightsizing, load balancing, and caching

knowledge.what_it_is: Prometheus is an open-source monitoring and alerting toolkit, primarily used for collecting metrics from applications and services, and providing real-time insights into system performance. It was created by SoundCloud and is now maintained by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF).
what_you_will_learn: Metrics collection — understanding how Prometheus collects metrics from applications and services using pull-based scraping or push-based protocols like Pushgateway.
Service discovery — learning about service discovery mechanisms in Prometheus, such as DNS, file-based, or environment variable-based configurations.
Alerting rules — creating alerting rules to notify teams of critical issues using PromQL queries, threshold-based alerts, and notification channels like email or PagerDuty.
PromQL — mastering the Prometheus Query Language (PromQL) for querying metrics, aggregating data, and generating alerts based on complex conditions.
Grafana integration — setting up Grafana as a visualization tool for Prometheus metrics, including creating dashboards, panels, and alerting rules.
Alertmanager configuration — configuring Alertmanager to manage notifications, silences, and routing of alerts from multiple sources.
Prometheus deployment strategies — understanding various deployment strategies for Prometheus, such as single-node, multi-node clusters, or hybrid approaches with other monitoring tools.

knowledge.what_it_is: Zabbix is an open-source IT monitoring tool that provides real-time monitoring and alerting capabilities for servers, networks, and applications. It supports a wide range of protocols, including SNMP, ICMP, TCP, and HTTP.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring Zabbix server and agent — setting up the Zabbix server and agent on Linux/Unix systems
Creating hosts and host groups in Zabbix — organizing monitored devices into logical groups for easier management
Defining triggers and actions in Zabbix — setting up custom alerting rules based on metric thresholds and taking automated actions
Monitoring CPU, memory, disk usage, and network traffic with Zabbix — tracking key system metrics to identify performance issues
Using Zabbix API for automation and integration — leveraging the Zabbix API to automate monitoring tasks and integrate with other tools
Setting up Zabbix web interface and user management — configuring the Zabbix web interface for users and setting up access controls
Configuring Zabbix to monitor cloud services like AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud — extending Zabbix monitoring capabilities to cloud environments

knowledge.what_it_is: Datadog is an AI-powered monitoring and analytics platform that provides real-time insights into application performance, infrastructure health, and security posture. It helps DevSecOps teams identify issues before they impact users and automate remediation.
what_you_will_learn: Agent installation — deploying Datadog agents on hosts for metrics collection
Configuring DogStatsD — sending custom metrics from applications to Datadog
Log collection — forwarding logs from various sources (files, containers, cloud providers) to Datadog
Dashboards and visualizations — creating custom dashboards with widgets, charts, and tables to monitor key performance indicators (KPIs)
Alerting and notification — setting up alerts based on metrics thresholds and configuring notifications via email, Slack, or PagerDuty
Synthetics monitoring — running automated browser and API tests to detect issues before they impact users
APM integration — instrumenting applications with Datadog APM libraries for distributed tracing and performance analysis

knowledge.what_it_is: Grafana is an open-source platform for building, managing, and visualizing metrics and logs from various data sources, widely used in DevSecOps for monitoring infrastructure performance, security, and compliance.
what_you_will_learn: Dashboards — creating custom dashboards with panels to visualize metrics and logs, using Grafana's drag-and-drop interface
Data Sources — connecting to various data sources such as Prometheus, InfluxDB, Elasticsearch, and more, using Grafana's plugin architecture
Queries — writing queries in PromQL or Graphite syntax to extract metrics from data sources, with support for filtering, grouping, and aggregation
Alerting — setting up alert rules based on metric thresholds, with notifications sent via email, Slack, PagerDuty, or other integrations
Security — configuring Grafana's security features, such as authentication, authorization, and access control, using plugins like OAuth, LDAP, or Active Directory
Logging — integrating Grafana with logging tools like ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana), Splunk, or Sumo Logic for log analysis and visualization

knowledge.what_it_is: Graylog is an open-source log management and analysis platform, designed to collect, store, and analyze log data from various sources. It provides real-time monitoring, alerting, and visualization capabilities for DevSecOps teams.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring Graylog inputs — setting up collectors to gather logs from various sources (e.g., filebeat, syslog)
Setting up Graylog outputs — configuring destinations for processed log data (e.g., Elasticsearch, Kafka)
Creating and managing Graylog streams — defining filters and rules for log processing and analysis
Using Graylog's alerting system — setting up notifications for critical events and anomalies
Configuring Graylog's visualization tools — creating dashboards and charts to monitor log data
Integrating Graylog with other DevSecOps tools — using APIs and plugins to integrate with CI/CD pipelines, monitoring systems, and security tools

knowledge.what_it_is: Cloud-specific tools are software applications designed to manage, monitor, and secure cloud-based infrastructure and services. They help DevSecOps teams ensure compliance, security, and efficiency in cloud environments.
what_you_will_learn: AWS CloudWatch — monitoring and logging service for AWS resources, including metrics, logs, and alarms
Azure Monitor — comprehensive monitoring and analytics platform for Azure resources, including performance, security, and troubleshooting
Google Cloud Logging — managed log analysis service for Google Cloud Platform resources, including ingestion, storage, and querying
CloudTrail — auditing and logging service for AWS resources, providing a detailed record of API calls and actions taken
Azure Policy — governance and compliance tool for Azure resources, enabling DevSecOps teams to define and enforce policies across the cloud environment
Google Cloud Audit Logs — security and compliance logs for Google Cloud Platform resources, including authentication, authorization, and data access events

knowledge.what_it_is: Papertrail is a cloud-based log management and analytics platform that helps DevSecOps teams monitor, analyze, and troubleshoot their applications and infrastructure. It supports various log formats, including Syslog, JSON, and CSV.
what_you_will_learn: Log collection — understanding how Papertrail collects logs from various sources, including servers, containers, and cloud services
Log filtering and routing — learning how to filter and route logs based on specific criteria, such as log level, source IP, or message content
Alerting and notification — configuring alerts and notifications for critical events, such as security breaches or performance issues
Log analysis and visualization — using Papertrail's built-in analytics and visualization tools to gain insights into application and infrastructure behavior
Integration with DevSecOps tools — understanding how to integrate Papertrail with other DevSecOps tools, such as Jenkins, Docker, and Kubernetes
Security features — learning about Papertrail's security features, including encryption, access controls, and audit logging

knowledge.what_it_is: Splunk is an enterprise-grade monitoring and analytics platform that enables DevSecOps teams to collect, index, and analyze machine-generated data from various sources. It provides real-time insights into system performance, security threats, and compliance issues.
what_you_will_learn: Data ingestion — collecting logs from various sources using Splunk's Universal Forwarder or Heavy Forwarder
Indexing and searching — understanding how Splunk indexes and searches data using its proprietary indexing algorithm
Event correlation — analyzing related events to identify security threats, performance issues, or compliance violations
Alerts and notifications — setting up alerts and notifications for critical events using Splunk's alerting system
Dashboards and visualizations — creating custom dashboards and visualizations to monitor key performance indicators (KPIs)
Splunk Query Language (SPL) — writing SPL queries to extract insights from indexed data
Data modeling — designing and implementing data models in Splunk to improve search efficiency and accuracy

knowledge.what_it_is: Loki is an open-source, highly scalable logging and monitoring system developed by Grafana Labs. It provides a unified way to collect, store, and visualize logs from various sources, making it easier to detect issues and troubleshoot applications.
what_you_will_learn: Prometheus-based metrics collection — understanding how Loki uses Prometheus to collect metrics
LogQL query language — learning the basics of LogQL for querying logs in Loki
Loki storage engine — understanding how Loki stores logs in a highly scalable and efficient manner
Distributed indexing — learning about Loki's distributed indexing mechanism for fast log retrieval
Multi-tenancy support — understanding how Loki supports multi-tenancy for large-scale deployments
Integration with Grafana — learning how to integrate Loki with Grafana for visualization and alerting
Alerting and notification — setting up alerts and notifications in Loki using PromQL and Alertmanager
Security features — understanding Loki's security features, such as authentication and authorization

knowledge.what_it_is: The Elastic Stack, also known as ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana), is a popular log management and analytics platform used by DevSecOps teams to collect, process, and visualize logs from various sources. It's designed for real-time monitoring, alerting, and troubleshooting of complex systems.
what_you_will_learn: Elasticsearch — a NoSQL search and analytics engine for storing and querying log data
Logstash — a data processing pipeline for collecting, transforming, and forwarding logs from various sources
Kibana — an interactive visualization tool for exploring and analyzing log data in Elasticsearch
Beats — lightweight shippers for sending logs to Logstash or directly to Elasticsearch
Filebeat — a Beats variant for shipping log files to Logstash or Elasticsearch
Metricbeat — a Beats variant for shipping system metrics to Elasticsearch
X-Pack Security — a security module for encrypting and authenticating access to Elasticsearch
Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) — managing user permissions and access control in Elasticsearch

knowledge.what_it_is: Container orchestration refers to the management of containerized applications and services across multiple hosts, ensuring efficient resource utilization, scalability, and high availability. In DevSecOps, container orchestration is crucial for automating deployment, scaling, and monitoring of microservices-based applications.
what_you_will_learn: Kubernetes — a popular open-source container orchestration system for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications
Pods — the basic execution unit in Kubernetes, comprising one or more containers that share resources and IP addresses
ReplicaSets — ensuring a specified number of replicas (pods) are running at any given time, providing high availability and scalability
Deployments — managing rollouts and rollbacks of new versions of applications, with features like blue-green deployments and canary releases
Services — abstracting pods and providing network identity and load balancing for accessing services from outside the cluster
Persistent Volumes (PVs) and Claims (PVCs) — managing storage resources for persistent data in Kubernetes
StatefulSets — managing stateful applications, such as databases, with features like ordered pod startup and rollout
DaemonSets — running one or more replicas of a pod across a cluster, typically used for system components like logging agents

knowledge.what_it_is: Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE), Amazon Elastic Container Service for Kubernetes (EKS), and Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) are managed container orchestration services that provide a scalable, secure, and highly available way to deploy and manage containerized applications in the cloud.
what_you_will_learn: Creating clusters — setting up GKE/EKS/AKS clusters using command-line tools or APIs
Node pools — managing node pools for optimal resource utilization and scalability
Pods and deployments — understanding pod lifecycle, deployment strategies (rolling updates, blue-green), and rollout management
Services and ingress — exposing applications to the internet using services and ingress controllers
Persistent volumes and storage classes — managing persistent data storage and classifying storage resources
Security and access control — configuring network policies, service accounts, and RBAC for secure cluster operation
Monitoring and logging — integrating with monitoring tools (Prometheus, Grafana) and logging solutions (Fluentd, ELK)
Cluster upgrades and maintenance — performing rolling updates, draining nodes, and handling node failures

knowledge.what_it_is: Amazon Elastic Container Service (ECS) and Amazon Elastic Container Service for Kubernetes (EKS) are container orchestration services offered by AWS. ECS allows you to run Docker containers on a managed cluster of EC2 instances, while EKS provides a managed Kubernetes service for running containerized applications. Fargate is a serverless compute engine that allows you to run containers without managing underlying infrastructure.
what_you_will_learn: Task definition — defining the resources and dependencies required by your container, including CPU, memory, and port mappings
Service discovery — using AWS Cloud Map or DNS to enable service registration and discovery within your cluster
Container networking — configuring network policies for your containers using AWS Network Load Balancer (NLB) or Amazon Elastic Container Service for Kubernetes (EKS)
Task placement strategies — defining how tasks are placed on EC2 instances, including spread, binpack, and random placement
Service scaling — automatically scaling the number of replicas based on CPU utilization or custom metrics using AWS CloudWatch
Rolling updates — gradually replacing old container versions with new ones to minimize downtime during deployment
Health checks — configuring health check policies for your containers, including HTTP, TCP, and command-based checks

knowledge.what_it_is: Docker Swarm is an open-source container orchestration tool that allows you to manage and deploy Docker containers across multiple hosts, providing high availability, load balancing, and scaling capabilities.
what_you_will_learn: Swarm Mode — enabling swarm mode on a Docker daemon to create a cluster of nodes
Service Discovery — using DNS-based service discovery for container communication within the swarm
Node Management — adding, removing, and managing nodes in the swarm cluster
Task Scheduling — scheduling tasks on available nodes based on resource utilization and affinity rules
Rolling Updates — performing rolling updates of services to minimize downtime during deployments
Secrets Management — storing and injecting secrets into containers using Docker Swarm's built-in secret management feature
Network Policies — defining network policies for container communication within the swarm, including ingress and egress traffic control

knowledge.what_it_is: Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration system for automating the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. It was originally designed by Google and is now maintained by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF). Kubernetes provides a declarative configuration model, allowing users to define the desired state of their application and letting the system handle the actual implementation.
what_you_will_learn: Pods — the basic execution unit in Kubernetes, comprising one or more containers
ReplicaSets — ensuring a specified number of replicas (pods) are running at any given time
Deployments — managing rollouts and rollbacks of new versions of an application
Services — providing a network identity and load balancing for accessing applications
Persistent Volumes (PVs) and Claims — persisting data across pod restarts and rescheduling
Namespaces — isolating resources and organizing cluster-wide policies
Labels and Selectors — filtering and selecting pods, services, and other resources
Resource Requests and Limits — managing resource allocation for pods and containers
Kubernetes Networking — understanding the different network models (ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer)
Kubernetes Security — configuring RBAC, Network Policies, and Secret Management
Kubernetes Storage — using Persistent Volumes, StatefulSets, and local storage options
Kubernetes Monitoring and Logging — integrating with tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and ELK
Kubernetes CI/CD Pipelines — automating deployments with Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, or CircleCI
Kubernetes Configuration Management — using Helm, Kustomize, or other templating engines

knowledge.what_it_is: Application monitoring is the process of tracking and analyzing application performance, errors, and user experience to ensure high availability, reliability, and security. It involves collecting data from various sources, such as logs, metrics, and traces, to provide real-time insights into application behavior.
what_you_will_learn: Prometheus — a popular open-source monitoring system for storing and querying metrics
Grafana — a visualization tool for creating dashboards and charts from Prometheus data
New Relic — an all-in-one monitoring platform for applications, infrastructure, and services
Distributed Tracing with Jaeger or OpenTelemetry — understanding request flow and latency across microservices
Error Tracking with Sentry or Rollbar — identifying and resolving application errors in real-time
Log Analysis with ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) or Splunk — extracting insights from log data
Service Level Objectives (SLOs) and Service Level Indicators (SLIs) — defining and measuring application performance targets
Anomaly Detection with machine learning algorithms or statistical methods — identifying unusual patterns in application behavior

knowledge.what_it_is: Jaeger is an open-source, distributed tracing system developed by Uber Technologies. It provides real-time monitoring and visualization of application performance, enabling DevSecOps teams to identify bottlenecks, optimize resource utilization, and ensure end-to-end visibility into complex systems.
what_you_will_learn: Distributed Tracing — the concept of tracing requests as they flow through a distributed system, including all services, APIs, and microservices
Jaeger Architecture — understanding the components of Jaeger, including the Collector, Agent, and Query Service
Service Map — visualizing service dependencies and communication patterns in real-time
Span — the basic unit of tracing in Jaeger, representing a single request or operation
Baggage — propagating context across services using a distributed key-value store
Jaeger UI — navigating and interpreting the Jaeger dashboard for end-to-end visibility into application performance

knowledge.what_it_is: New Relic is an observability platform that provides real-time insights into application performance, infrastructure, and user experience. It offers a comprehensive suite of tools for monitoring, troubleshooting, and optimizing complex systems.
what_you_will_learn: Agent installation — deploying New Relic agents on various platforms (Linux, Windows, Docker) to collect metrics and logs
Metric collection — understanding how New Relic collects and processes custom metrics, including data types and retention policies
Log management — configuring log collection, filtering, and forwarding to New Relic from various sources (files, network devices)
Dashboards and charts — creating custom dashboards and visualizations using New Relic's UI and API
Alerting and notifications — setting up alert conditions, notification channels, and escalation policies in New Relic
Synthetics monitoring — configuring synthetic monitors to simulate user interactions and measure application performance
Error tracking — analyzing and debugging errors with New Relic's error tracking features
Infrastructure monitoring — discovering and monitoring infrastructure resources (servers, containers, databases) using New Relic

knowledge.what_it_is: OpenTelemetry is an open-source observability framework that provides a unified API and SDKs for collecting, processing, and exporting telemetry data. It's designed to work with cloud-native applications and microservices, and supports multiple data formats and backends.
what_you_will_learn: Instrumentation — using OpenTelemetry SDKs to instrument code and collect metrics, logs, and traces
Tracing — understanding how OpenTelemetry implements distributed tracing, including propagation of context and correlation IDs
Metrics — collecting and exporting custom metrics using OpenTelemetry's metric API
Logs — integrating with log aggregation tools like Fluentd or Logstash to export logs from OpenTelemetry
Context Propagation — using OpenTelemetry's context propagation APIs to share data between services and components
Service Map — visualizing service dependencies and communication patterns using OpenTelemetry's Service Map feature
Distributed Tracing with B3 Headers — understanding how OpenTelemetry uses B3 headers for distributed tracing
OpenTelemetry Collector — configuring the collector to export telemetry data to various backends, including Prometheus, Grafana, and Splunk

knowledge.what_it_is: CloudSmith is an open-source, multi-cloud infrastructure management platform that enables DevSecOps teams to automate and secure their cloud resources across AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, and Kubernetes. It provides a unified view of cloud assets, automates compliance checks, and integrates with popular CI/CD tools.
what_you_will_learn: CloudSmith CLI — using the command-line interface to manage cloud resources, create infrastructure as code (IaC) templates, and automate deployments
CloudFormation Templates — creating AWS CloudFormation templates for provisioning and managing AWS resources within CloudSmith
Kubernetes Configuration — configuring Kubernetes clusters within CloudSmith to deploy containerized applications
Azure Resource Manager (ARM) Templates — using ARM templates to provision and manage Azure resources within CloudSmith
Google Cloud Deployment Manager — using Google Cloud Deployment Manager to create and manage infrastructure as code in CloudSmith
CloudSmith Policies — creating custom policies to enforce compliance checks, automate remediation tasks, and integrate with existing security tools
CI/CD Pipeline Integration — integrating CloudSmith with popular CI/CD tools like Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, and CircleCI for automated deployments and testing
Monitoring and Logging — configuring monitoring and logging integrations within CloudSmith to track cloud resource performance and troubleshoot issues

knowledge.what_it_is: Artifactory is a universal repository manager that enables DevSecOps teams to manage, share, and reuse artifacts across various development stages. It supports multiple formats, including Maven, Gradle, npm, and NuGet.
what_you_will_learn: Repository creation — setting up Artifactory repositories for different artifact types (e.g., Maven, npm)
Artifact management — uploading, downloading, and managing artifacts across various development stages
Dependency management — resolving dependencies using Artifactory's built-in dependency resolver
Proxy configuration — configuring Artifactory as a proxy for external repositories to improve performance and security
User authentication and authorization — setting up user authentication and authorization mechanisms in Artifactory
Audit logging and reporting — monitoring and analyzing audit logs to ensure compliance and security
Integration with CI/CD tools — integrating Artifactory with popular CI/CD tools like Jenkins, GitLab CI/CD, and CircleCI

knowledge.what_it_is: Nexus is an open-source, self-contained artifact repository manager that automates build, test, and deployment processes across multiple projects and teams. It supports both Maven and Gradle repositories, and provides features like automated builds, snapshot dependencies, and role-based access control.
what_you_will_learn: Repository management — creating, managing, and searching artifacts in Nexus
Maven repository integration — configuring and using Nexus as a Maven repository
Gradle repository integration — configuring and using Nexus as a Gradle repository
Snapshot dependencies — understanding how to use snapshot versions of dependencies in Nexus
Role-based access control — setting up user roles and permissions for secure artifact management
Automated builds — triggering builds in Nexus based on changes in the source code or other events
Repository proxying — configuring Nexus as a proxy repository for external repositories
Artifact promotion — understanding how to promote artifacts from one environment to another

knowledge.what_it_is: FluxCD is a popular open-source Continuous Delivery (CD) tool, designed to automate software delivery and deployment processes. It provides a declarative configuration model, allowing users to define desired application states without worrying about the underlying infrastructure.
what_you_will_learn: GitOps — managing infrastructure as code with Git repositories, enabling version control and collaboration
FluxCD components — understanding the core concepts of sources, reconcilers, and controllers for automating CD pipelines
Kustomize overlays — applying custom configurations to Kubernetes resources using Kustomize's overlay mechanism
Git repository management — managing FluxCD configuration files in Git repositories, including source control and versioning
Cluster bootstrapping — initializing a new cluster with FluxCD, including setting up the Control Plane and worker nodes
FluxCD Helm charts — deploying FluxCD using Helm charts for easy installation and management
Monitoring and logging integration — integrating FluxCD with monitoring and logging tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and ELK Stack

knowledge.what_it_is: ArgoCD is an open-source, declarative Continuous Deployment (CD) tool that automates the delivery of applications and infrastructure to Kubernetes clusters. It provides a GitOps approach to CD, enabling teams to manage complex workflows and ensure consistency across environments.
what_you_will_learn: Kubernetes manifests — defining application resources using YAML/JSON files
Git repositories — storing and managing application code and configurations in version control
Application configuration management — using ArgoCD's built-in features to manage app configs, secrets, and env vars
Source-to-Image (S2I) pipelines — automating the build process for containerized applications
Canary releases — rolling out new versions of apps with minimal risk using ArgoCD's deployment strategies
Blue-green deployments — seamlessly switching between old and new app versions without downtime
ArgoCD CLI — using the command-line interface to manage CD workflows, sync apps, and monitor deployments

knowledge.what_it_is: Istio is an open-source service mesh platform that provides a layer of abstraction and automation for microservices communication, security, and observability. It was created by Google, IBM, and Lyft to address the complexities of modern cloud-native applications.
what_you_will_learn: Service Mesh Architecture — understanding Istio's role in creating a service mesh and its components (Pilot, Galley, Citadel)
Istio Components — learning about the different components that make up Istio, including Pilot, Galley, Citadel, Mixer, and Sidecar
Traffic Management — configuring routing rules, canary releases, and traffic shifting using Istio's built-in features
Security Features — implementing mTLS encryption, role-based access control (RBAC), and secret management with Istio
Service Discovery — understanding how Istio discovers services and instances, including DNS, Kube-DNS, and service registries
Observability — using Istio's built-in features for monitoring, logging, and tracing microservices, including Prometheus, Grafana, and Jaeger
Istio Configuration — learning about the different ways to configure Istio, including YAML files, Kubernetes manifests, and the Istio CLI

knowledge.what_it_is: Consul is a popular service mesh and configuration management tool developed by HashiCorp, used to manage and secure microservices-based applications.
what_you_will_learn: Service discovery — registering and discovering services within the Consul cluster
Health checking — monitoring service health and triggering automatic failover or scaling
Service registration — dynamically registering services with Consul for automatic discovery
Consul configuration — managing application configurations through Consul's key-value store
Secret management — securely storing and retrieving sensitive data using Consul's built-in secrets manager
Service mesh integration — integrating Consul with service mesh frameworks like Istio or Linkerd
Consul Connect — establishing secure communication between services using mutual TLS authentication
Consul Catalog — managing service instances and their associated metadata within the Consul cluster

knowledge.what_it_is: Linkerd is a modern service mesh that provides features like traffic management, security, and observability for microservices-based applications. It was created by Buoyant and is now part of the CNCF (Cloud Native Computing Foundation). Linkerd is designed to be highly scalable, flexible, and easy to use.
what_you_will_learn: Service mesh architecture — understanding how Linkerd fits into a larger microservices ecosystem
Traffic management — using Linkerd's built-in traffic shifting, circuit breaking, and request routing features
MTC (Microgateway Traffic Control) — configuring fine-grained traffic control for individual services
L5D (Linkerd 5 Distributed) — understanding the distributed architecture of Linkerd and its implications for scalability
Service identity and discovery — using Linkerd's service registry and DNS-based service discovery
Security features — implementing authentication, authorization, and encryption with Linkerd's built-in security features
Observability tools — integrating Prometheus, Grafana, and Jaeger with Linkerd for monitoring and troubleshooting
Linkerd CLI and API — understanding the command-line interface and RESTful API for managing Linkerd clusters

knowledge.what_it_is: Envoy is an open-source service mesh platform developed by Lyft, designed to provide a flexible and extensible way to manage service communication, security, and observability in modern microservices architectures. It supports multiple programming languages, including Java, Python, and Go.
what_you_will_learn: Service discovery — Envoy's built-in support for service registration and discovery using etcd or Consul
Traffic management — routing traffic between services using Envoy's xDS APIs and gRPC
Rate limiting and circuit breaking — implementing rate limits and circuit breakers to prevent cascading failures
Authentication and authorization — integrating Envoy with external auth providers like OAuth2, JWT, and LDAP
Service mesh configuration — managing Envoy configurations using tools like Helm, Kubernetes ConfigMaps, or etcd
Observability and logging — integrating Envoy with observability tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and ELK Stack

knowledge.what_it_is: Availability refers to the measure of how often a system or service is operational and accessible, ensuring that it meets its performance and reliability requirements. In DevSecOps, availability is crucial for delivering high-quality software quickly and reliably.
what_you_will_learn: High Availability (HA) clusters — designing and implementing HA clusters using tools like Pacemaker, Corosync, or Keepalived to ensure system uptime
Load Balancing — configuring load balancers like HAProxy, NGINX, or Amazon ELB to distribute traffic and prevent single points of failure
Auto Scaling — setting up auto scaling groups in AWS, GCP, or Azure to dynamically adjust resource capacity based on demand
Disaster Recovery (DR) planning — creating DR plans using tools like Zerto, Veeam, or VMware Site Recovery Manager to ensure business continuity
Service Level Agreement (SLA) management — defining and monitoring SLAs for system availability, response times, and other performance metrics
Monitoring and logging — setting up monitoring tools like Prometheus, Grafana, or New Relic to track system performance and detect potential issues
Capacity planning — analyzing system capacity requirements using tools like Capacity Planner or RightScale to ensure adequate resources are allocated

knowledge.what_it_is: Data management in DevSecOps refers to the practices and tools used to collect, store, process, and analyze data related to infrastructure, applications, and security. It involves using various technologies such as monitoring tools, logging frameworks, and data warehouses to gain insights and make informed decisions.
what_you_will_learn: Data collection — gathering logs, metrics, and other data from various sources using tools like Fluentd, Logstash, or Splunk
Data storage — designing and implementing databases such as PostgreSQL, MySQL, or MongoDB for storing and managing large datasets
Data processing — using frameworks like Apache Spark, Flink, or Beam to process and transform data in real-time
Data analysis — applying statistical techniques and machine learning algorithms using libraries like Pandas, NumPy, or scikit-learn to gain insights from data
Data visualization — creating interactive dashboards and reports using tools like Grafana, Kibana, or Tableau to communicate findings to stakeholders
Monitoring and alerting — setting up monitoring systems like Prometheus, Grafana, or New Relic to detect anomalies and trigger alerts for incident response
Logging best practices — implementing secure logging practices such as encryption, access control, and log rotation using tools like Logrotate or Rsyslog

knowledge.what_it_is: Cloud Design Patterns are reusable solutions to common problems encountered when designing cloud-based systems, providing a framework for architects and engineers to create scalable, secure, and efficient cloud infrastructure.
what_you_will_learn: Serverless Architecture — designing applications that run on event-driven serverless platforms like AWS Lambda or Google Cloud Functions
Microservices Design — breaking down monolithic applications into smaller, independent services communicating through APIs and message queues
Service Mesh Patterns — using service mesh technologies like Istio or Linkerd to manage communication between microservices and enforce policies
Event-Driven Architecture — designing systems that respond to events triggered by external sources or internal state changes
CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) Pattern — separating read and write operations into different models for improved scalability and performance
API Gateway Patterns — using API gateways like AWS API Gateway or Google Cloud Endpoints to manage API traffic and security
Security-as-Code Patterns — integrating security practices into the development process through tools like Terraform or Ansible

knowledge.what_it_is: Management and Monitoring in DevSecOps refers to the practices and tools used to ensure the reliability, performance, and security of cloud-based infrastructure and applications. This includes monitoring system metrics, logs, and application performance, as well as implementing automation and orchestration to manage infrastructure changes.
what_you_will_learn: Prometheus — a popular open-source monitoring system for collecting and storing metrics from various sources
Grafana — a visualization tool for creating dashboards to display Prometheus metrics and logs
New Relic — an application performance monitoring (APM) tool that provides insights into application performance and user experience
CloudWatch — AWS's cloud-based monitoring service for tracking system metrics, logs, and application performance
Dynatrace — a comprehensive APM tool that provides real-time monitoring of application performance and user experience
Kubernetes Dashboard — a web-based interface for managing Kubernetes clusters and monitoring pod performance
ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) — a popular log management solution for collecting, storing, and visualizing logs
Splunk — a comprehensive logging and monitoring platform that provides real-time insights into system metrics and application performance
